results.label: German hospital — multi-stage actor status transition (MDR 5(5) + AI Act provider/deployer)

full.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination.

> ⚠ BACKBONE FLAG — This answer may incorrectly classify the original AI system developer as a deployer. Under Article 3(3) AI Act, development + internal deployment = provider status from inception. Verify the initial-status analysis before relying on this answer.

### 1. Initial Stage (Development & Internal Use Under MDR Article 5(5) Exemption)
#### MDR 2017/745
- Actor Status: The German university hospital is a manufacturer under **Article 2(30)** of the MDR because it designs and develops the AI-assisted pathology system for its own use. However, it qualifies for the health institution exemption under **Article 5(5)** because:
 - The device is manufactured and used only within the hospital (**Article 5(5)**(a)).
 - The hospital justifies that no equivalent device is available on the market (**Article 5(5)**(c)).
 - The hospital maintains appropriate quality management systems (**Article 5(5)**(b)) and documentation (**Article 5(5)**(f)).
 - The device is not transferred to another legal entity (**Article 5(5)**(a)).

 Conclusion: The hospital remains a manufacturer but is exempt from most MDR obligations (except **Annex I** general safety and performance requirements).

#### EU AI Act
- Actor Status: The hospital is a provider under **Article 3(3)** of the AI Act because:
 - It develops the AI system in-house.
 - It puts the AI system into service under its own name for internal use.
 - No in-house exemption exists in the AI Act—the hospital is a provider from inception, regardless of whether it places the system on the market.

- Classification Route:
 - The AI system is high-risk under **Article 6(1)** because:
 - It is a safety component of a medical device (pathology system) covered by **Annex I**, Section A, point 11 (MDR 2017/745).
 - The medical device (pathology system) requires third-party conformity assessment (assuming it is Class IIa, IIb, or III under MDR).
 - No derogation applies to **Article 6(1)** high-risk classification.

 Conclusion: The hospital is a provider of a high-risk AI system and must comply with Chapter III, Section 2 obligations (e.g., risk management, technical documentation, conformity assessment).

---

### 2. Stage 2 (Continuous Learning Pipeline Introduced)
#### MDR 2017/745
- Impact on Exemption:
 - The **Article 5(5)** exemption remains valid if:
 - The retraining logic, validation thresholds, and spectral domains were pre-defined in the initial technical documentation (**Article 5(5)**(f)).
 - The quality management system (QMS) ensures compliance with **Annex I** requirements (**Article 5(5)**(b)).
 - The device is still used only within the hospital (**Article 5(5)**(a)).
 - Risk of losing exemption: If the continuous learning introduces substantial modifications (e.g., new intended purpose, significant changes to performance or safety), the hospital may no longer meet **Article 5(5)**(c) (justification of unmet patient needs) and could lose the exemption.

 Conclusion: The hospital likely retains the exemption if the modifications are pre-defined and controlled under its QMS.

#### EU AI Act
- Impact on Provider Status:
 - The hospital remains a provider under **Article 3(3)**.
 - High-risk classification persists because:
 - The AI system remains a safety component of a medical device (MDR **Annex I**).
 - The continuous learning does not alter the intended purpose (tumor margin classification).
 - Obligations triggered:
 - Post-market monitoring (**Article 72**): The provider must monitor performance and report serious incidents.
 - Technical documentation updates (**Article 11**): The hospital must document the retraining pipeline and validation results.
 - Conformity assessment (**Article 43**): If the modifications are substantial, a new conformity assessment may be required.

 Conclusion: The hospital remains a high-risk AI provider and must update documentation and monitoring but does not transition to a deployer.

---

### 3. Stage 3 (Collaboration with French Hospital – Model Weights Shared)
#### MDR 2017/745
- Impact on Exemption:
 - **Article 5(5)**(a) is violated: The transfer of model weights to another legal entity (French hospital) is likely to constitute a "transfer to another legal entity", which invalidates the exemption.
 - Consequence: The German hospital loses the **Article 5(5)** exemption and becomes a full MDR manufacturer with all obligations, including:
 - Conformity assessment (if Class IIa/IIb/III).
 - Technical documentation (**Annex II**).
 - Post-market surveillance (**Article 83**).
 - UDI registration (**Article 27**).

 Conclusion: The transfer is likely to trigger a transition from exempt manufacturer to full MDR manufacturer.

#### EU AI Act
- Impact on Provider Status:
 - The German hospital remains a provider under **Article 3(3)** because:
 - It developed the AI system and continues to control its updates.
 - The French hospital is a deployer under **Article 3(4)** (uses the system under its authority).
 - no clear transition to deployer: The German hospital does not become a deployer because it did not receive the system from a third-party provider.
 - Obligations triggered:
 - Conformity assessment (**Article 43**): If the sharing of model weights is likely to constitute a "placing on the market", the hospital must ensure compliance with AI Act requirements (e.g., EU declaration of conformity, technical documentation).
 - Transparency obligations (**Article 50**): If the AI system interacts with natural persons, the provider must inform users they are interacting with an AI system.
 - Post-market monitoring (**Article 72**): The provider must monitor performance across both hospitals.

 Conclusion: The German hospital remains a high-risk AI provider and must comply with additional obligations due to the cross-border deployment.

---

### Synthesis Table
| Stage | MDR 2017/745 | EU AI Act |
|-------------------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Initial Development | Manufacturer (exempt under **Article 5(5)**) | Provider (high-risk under **Article 6(1)**) |
| Continuous Learning | Exemption likely retained | Provider (high-risk, updated obligations) |
| Model Weights Shared| Loses exemption → Full manufacturer | Remains provider (deployer in France) |

### Bottom-Line Conclusions
1. MDR Transition Trigger: The transfer of model weights to the French hospital causes the German hospital to lose the **Article 5(5)** exemption and become a full MDR manufacturer.
2. AI Act Status: The German hospital remains a provider under the AI Act at all stages. The French hospital is a deployer.
3. Key Obligations:
 - MDR: Conformity assessment, technical documentation, PMS, UDI registration.
 - AI Act: Conformity assessment (if placing on the market), post-market monitoring, transparency obligations.
4. Cross-Regulatory Dependency:
 - The MDR classification (Class IIa/IIb/III) determines whether the AI system is high-risk under AI Act **Article 6(1)**.
 - The AI Act imposes additional obligations (e.g., risk management, technical documentation) that complement MDR requirements.

### Residual Uncertainty
- Substantial Modification: If the continuous learning introduces unforeseen changes, the hospital may need to reassess MDR classification and AI Act conformity.
- Placing on the Market: If the sharing of model weights is considered "placing on the market", the German hospital must comply with AI Act conformity assessment procedures (e.g., EU declaration of conformity).
- French Hospital’s Role: The French hospital’s status (deployer vs. provider) depends on whether it modifies the system (e.g., retrains with local data). If it does, it may become a provider under **Article 25**.

flat.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment with partial retrieval support. Treat conclusions below as tentative and case-specific unless directly quoted from the retrieved provisions.

### 1. Initial Stage: Development and Internal Use (Before Continuous Learning or Collaboration)

#### MDR 2017/745 – Manufacturer Status
- The German university hospital is a manufacturer under **Article 2(30)** MDR from the outset because it designs and manufactures the AI-assisted pathology system for its own use.
- However, **Article 5(5)** MDR exempts the hospital from most MDR obligations (e.g., conformity assessment, UDI registration, post-market surveillance reporting) provided all conditions are met, including:
 - The device is not transferred to another legal entity (**Article 5(5)**(a)).
 - The hospital justifies that no equivalent device is available on the market (**Article 5(5)**(c)).
 - The hospital maintains appropriate quality management systems (**Article 5(5)**(b)) and documentation (**Article 5(5)**(f)).
 - The hospital reviews clinical experience and takes corrective actions (**Article 5(5)**(h)).
- Key point: The exemption does not negate manufacturer status—it only relieves the hospital of certain obligations while still requiring compliance with **Annex I** (general safety and performance requirements).

#### EU AI Act – Provider Status
- The hospital is a provider under **Article 3(3)** AI Act from the moment it develops the AI system and puts it into service under its own name.
 - The AI Act does not have an in-house exemption for providers. Even if the system is used internally, the hospital is the original developer and thus the provider (**Article 3(3)**).
 - **Article 25** AI Act (responsibilities along the AI value chain) does not apply here because the hospital is the initial developer, not a third party modifying an existing system.
- High-risk classification:
 - The system is high-risk under **Article 6(1)** AI Act because:
 1. It is a safety component of a medical device (pathology system used during surgery).
 2. The medical device (the AI-assisted pathology system) is subject to third-party conformity assessment under MDR (likely Class IIa or higher, given its use in tumor margin classification).
 - Route I (**Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**) applies, not Route II (**Annex III**), because the system is integrated into a medical device regulated under MDR.

#### Summary of Initial Stage
| Regulation | Legal Status of Hospital | Obligations |
|------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| MDR | Manufacturer | Exempt from most obligations (**Article 5(5)**), but must comply with **Annex I** and maintain QMS/documentation. |
| AI Act | Provider | Full high-risk provider obligations (**Article 16**, **Article 9**, **Article 11**, etc.). No exemption for in-house use. |

---

### 2. Stage 2: Introduction of Continuous Learning Pipeline

#### MDR 2017/745 – Impact on Manufacturer Status and Exemption
- The continuous learning pipeline (weekly retraining) does not clearly trigger a loss of the **Article 5(5)** exemption if:
 - The retraining is pre-defined in the initial technical documentation (e.g., validation thresholds, spectral domains).
 - The hospital maintains control over the system and does not transfer it to another legal entity (**Article 5(5)**(a)).
 - The hospital reviews clinical experience and takes corrective actions (**Article 5(5)**(h)).
- Risk of exemption loss:
 - If the retraining substantially changes the system’s performance, safety profile, or intended purpose, the hospital may no longer meet the **Article 5(5)**(c) justification (no equivalent device available).
 - The hospital must document that the retraining does not compromise compliance with **Annex I** (general safety and performance requirements).

#### EU AI Act – Impact on Provider Status
- The continuous learning pipeline does not convert the hospital into a new provider because:
 - The hospital remains the original developer and provider (**Article 3(3)**).
 - The retraining is pre-defined and does not constitute a substantial modification (**Article 3(23)**), which would require a new conformity assessment.
- Obligations triggered by continuous learning:
 - The hospital must update technical documentation (**Article 11(1)**) to reflect changes in the model’s performance.
 - The post-market monitoring system (**Article 72**) must track the impact of retraining on safety and performance.
 - The hospital must assess risks (**Article 9**) arising from the retraining and implement mitigations.

#### Summary of Stage 2
| Regulation | Legal Status of Hospital | Key Considerations |
|------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| MDR | Manufacturer | Exemption remains if retraining is pre-defined and does not compromise safety/performance. |
| AI Act | Provider | No change in status, but technical documentation and risk management must be updated. |

---

### 3. Stage 3: Collaboration with French Hospital (Model Weight Sharing)

#### MDR 2017/745 – Loss of Exemption
- Transfer of model weights to the French hospital = transfer to another legal entity (**Article 5(5)**(a)).
 - This immediately terminates the **Article 5(5)** exemption.
 - The German hospital retains manufacturer status and must now comply with all MDR obligations, including:
 - Conformity assessment (likely **Annex IX** or X, depending on risk class).
 - UDI registration (**Article 27**, **Annex VI**).
 - Post-market surveillance (**Article 83**).
 - Serious incident reporting (**Article 87**).
 - The French hospital does not become a manufacturer unless it modifies the system or rebrands it (**Article 16** MDR).

#### EU AI Act – Provider Status Transition
- German hospital remains the provider under **Article 3(3)** because:
 - It developed the system and controls the intended purpose.
 - The transfer of model weights alone does not constitute placing on the market (**Article 3(10)**) or putting into service (**Article 3(11)**) by the French hospital.
- French hospital’s status:
 - The French hospital is a deployer (**Article 3(4)**) because it uses the system under its authority but did not develop it.
 - If the French hospital modifies the system (e.g., retrains it with its own data), it could become a new provider under **Article 25(1)**(b) (substantial modification).
- Obligations triggered by collaboration:
 - The German hospital must update technical documentation (**Article 11**) to reflect the collaboration.
 - The German hospital must ensure the French hospital has instructions for use (**Article 13**).
 - The French hospital must monitor the system’s performance (**Article 26(5)**) and report risks to the German hospital.

#### Summary of Stage 3
| Regulation | Legal Status of German Hospital | Legal Status of French Hospital | Key Obligations |
|------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| MDR | Manufacturer | Not a manufacturer | German hospital loses **Article 5(5)** exemption and must comply with full MDR obligations. |
| AI Act | Provider | Deployer | German hospital remains provider; French hospital is deployer unless it modifies the system. |

---

### 4. Synthesis: Transition Points

| Event | MDR Transition | AI Act Transition |
|------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Initial development | Manufacturer (exempt under **Article 5(5)**) | Provider (**Article 3(3)**) |
| Continuous learning (pre-defined) | No change (exemption retained) | No change (provider retains obligations) |
| Transfer to French hospital | Loss of **Article 5(5)** exemption | No change (German hospital remains provider) |
| French hospital modifies system | French hospital becomes manufacturer | French hospital becomes new provider (**Article 25(1)**(b)) |

---

### 5. Key Takeaways
1. MDR Manufacturer Status:
 - The hospital is a manufacturer from the outset but is exempt under **Article 5(5)** as long as the system is not transferred to another legal entity.
 - Transferring model weights to the French hospital terminates the exemption, requiring full MDR compliance.

2. AI Act Provider Status:
 - The hospital is a provider from the outset (**Article 3(3)**) with no in-house exemption.
 - Continuous learning does not change provider status if pre-defined.
 - Transferring model weights does not change provider status unless the French hospital modifies the system.

3. Cross-Regulation Interaction:
 - The AI Act’s high-risk classification (via **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**) is independent of the MDR exemption.
 - The MDR exemption does not apply to AI Act obligations—the hospital must comply with both regulations where applicable.

4. Critical Compliance Steps:
 - Before transfer: Ensure the system meets MDR **Annex I** and AI Act high-risk requirements.
 - After transfer: The German hospital must register the device, undergo conformity assessment, and comply with post-market surveillance under MDR.
 - French hospital: Must comply with deployer obligations under the AI Act unless it modifies the system.

results.label: Benchmark — Class IIb SaMD with continuous learning, PROVIDER obligations (AI Act + MDR)

full.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination.

### 1. Actor Status
#### Under the EU AI Act
Your company is the provider of the AI system under **Article 3(3)** of the AI Act because you develop the AI system and place it on the market under your own name or trademark. This status applies regardless of whether the system is deployed internally or externally, and it remains unchanged even if the system exhibits continuous-learning behaviour after deployment.

#### Under the MDR 2017/745
You are the manufacturer of the medical device under **Article 2(1)** of the MDR, as you are the entity responsible for the design, development, and placing on the market of the software with a medical purpose.

---

### 2. Classification Route
#### AI Act High-Risk Classification
Your AI system qualifies as high-risk under **Article 6(1)** of the AI Act via Route I (**Annex I**) because:
1. It is software as a medical device (SaMD), which is explicitly covered by Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR) listed in **Annex I**, Section A, point 11 of the AI Act.
2. As a Class IIb medical device, it is subject to third-party conformity assessment under the MDR (required for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices). This satisfies the second condition of **Article 6(1)**(b).

Route II (**Annex III**) does not apply unless your system also performs a function explicitly listed in **Annex III** (e.g., biometric categorisation, essential services, or law enforcement), which is not indicated in your scenario.

#### MDR Classification
Your device is already classified as Class IIb under the MDR, which determines the applicable conformity assessment procedure and lifecycle obligations.

---

### 3. Trigger Event
The obligations under both regulations are triggered by:
- Placing the device on the market (first making available in the EU for commercial purposes) under **Article 2(28)** MDR and **Article 3(10)** AI Act.
- Putting the device into service (first use by the final user for its intended purpose) under **Article 2(29)** MDR and **Article 3(11)** AI Act.
- Continuous-learning behaviour after deployment, which engages additional post-market monitoring and risk management obligations under both regulations.

---

### 4. Legal Effects: Obligation Clusters
#### AI Act Obligations for High-Risk AI Systems
As the provider of a high-risk AI system, you must comply with the following obligations under Chapter III, Section 2 and Section 3 of the AI Act:

| Obligation | Governing Provision | Key Requirements |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Compliance with high-risk requirements | **Article 8(1)** and Section 2 (**Articles 9**–15) | Ensure the system meets accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, data governance, transparency, and human oversight requirements. |
| Risk management system | **Article 9** | Establish a continuous iterative process to identify, analyse, and mitigate risks throughout the lifecycle, including those arising from continuous learning. |
| Data governance | **Article 10** | Ensure training, validation, and testing datasets are relevant, representative, and free from errors/bias. Document data collection processes and justify dataset choices. |
| Technical documentation | **Article 11** and **Annex IV** | Maintain comprehensive documentation, including system design, risk management, data governance, and post-market monitoring plans. |
| Record-keeping (automated logs) | **Article 12** | Implement logging capabilities to ensure traceability of the system’s operation, including continuous-learning updates. |
| Transparency and human oversight | **Article 13** and **Article 14** | Provide clear instructions for use, including limitations, performance metrics, and human oversight mechanisms. Ensure the system is designed to enable effective human oversight, particularly for continuous-learning behaviour. |
| Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity | **Article 15** | Ensure the system achieves appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness, including resilience to errors, faults, and adversarial attacks. Address risks of feedback loops (e.g., biased outputs influencing future inputs) in continuous-learning models. |
| Quality management system (QMS) | **Article 17** | Implement a QMS covering regulatory compliance, design control, development procedures, risk management, post-market monitoring, and corrective actions. |
| Conformity assessment | **Article 43(3)** | Follow the MDR conformity assessment procedure (since the AI system is a medical device). The AI Act requirements (Section 2) are integrated into this assessment. Points 4.3, 4.4, 4.5, and 4.6 of **Annex VII** of the AI Act also apply. |
| EU declaration of conformity | **Article 47** | Draw up and maintain an EU declaration of conformity, including for the AI system’s compliance with Section 2 of the AI Act. |
| CE marking | **Article 48** | Affix the CE marking to the device or its documentation to indicate compliance with both the MDR and AI Act. |
| Registration | **Article 49(1)** | Register the high-risk AI system in the EU database before placing it on the market or putting it into service. |
| Post-market monitoring (PMM) | **Article 72** | Establish a post-market monitoring system to actively collect and review data on the system’s performance, including continuous-learning behaviour. Report serious incidents and corrective actions to competent authorities. |
| Serious incident reporting | **Article 73** | Report serious incidents (e.g., malfunctions causing harm) to market surveillance authorities without undue delay. |
| Corrective actions | **Article 20** | Take corrective actions (e.g., recalls, updates) if the system is found to be non-compliant or presents risks. Inform distributors, deployers, and authorities. |
| Cooperation with authorities | **Article 21** | Provide technical documentation and logs to competent authorities upon request. |
| AI literacy | **Article 4** | Ensure your staff and users have sufficient AI literacy to operate and oversee the system, particularly for continuous-learning models. |

#### MDR Obligations for Class IIb Medical Devices
As the manufacturer of a Class IIb medical device, you must comply with the following MDR obligations, which overlap with and complement the AI Act requirements:

| Obligation | Governing Provision | Key Requirements |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| General obligations of manufacturers | **Article 10** | Ensure the device meets MDR requirements, including risk management, clinical evaluation, technical documentation, and post-market surveillance. |
| Risk management | **Annex I**, Chapter I | Implement a risk management system (ISO 14971) to identify and mitigate risks, including those arising from continuous learning. |
| Clinical evaluation | **Article 61** and **Annex XIV** | Conduct a clinical evaluation to demonstrate conformity with safety and performance requirements. For continuous-learning models, this includes ongoing performance monitoring and updates to the clinical evaluation. |
| Technical documentation | **Annex II** and III | Maintain technical documentation, including design, risk management, clinical evaluation, and post-market surveillance data. |
| Post-market surveillance (PMS) | **Article 83** and **Annex III** | Establish a PMS system to monitor the device’s performance and safety, including continuous-learning behaviour. Update the PMS plan and report as required. |
| Post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF) | **Article 84** and **Annex XIV**, Part B | Conduct PMCF to proactively collect clinical data on the device’s performance, particularly for continuous-learning models. |
| Vigilance reporting | **Article 87** | Report serious incidents and field safety corrective actions (FSCAs) to competent authorities. |
| UDI and registration | **Article 27** and **Article 29** | Assign a Unique Device Identifier (UDI) and register the device in EUDAMED. |
| Conformity assessment | **Annex IX** or X | Follow the Class IIb conformity assessment procedure, which requires involvement of a notified body. The AI Act requirements (Section 2) are integrated into this assessment. |
| EU declaration of conformity | **Article 19** | Draw up an EU declaration of conformity, which may be combined with the AI Act declaration under **Article 47(3)** AI Act. |
| CE marking | **Article 20** | Affix the CE marking to the device to indicate compliance with the MDR. |

---

### 5. Cross-Regulatory Chain: Interaction Between AI Act and MDR
The AI Act and MDR interact as follows for your Class IIb continuous-learning SaMD:

1. Conformity Assessment:
 - The MDR conformity assessment procedure (e.g., **Annex IX** or X) applies to your device as a Class IIb medical device.
 - The AI Act requirements (Section 2) are integrated into this assessment under **Article 43(3)** AI Act. This means the notified body assessing your device under the MDR will also verify compliance with the AI Act’s high-risk requirements.
 - You do not need a separate AI Act conformity assessment procedure (e.g., **Annex VI** or VII) unless your system also falls under **Annex III** of the AI Act, which is not the case here.

2. Post-Market Monitoring (PMM) and Surveillance:
 - The MDR PMS system (**Article 83**) and AI Act PMM system (**Article 72**) are complementary. You must establish a single, integrated system to monitor the device’s performance, including continuous-learning behaviour, and report serious incidents under both regulations.
 - The AI Act’s PMM plan must address risks specific to AI, such as feedback loops, bias, and model drift, while the MDR PMS plan focuses on clinical safety and performance.

3. Clinical Evaluation and Performance Monitoring:
 - The MDR clinical evaluation (**Article 61**) must include an assessment of the AI system’s performance, including its continuous-learning behaviour.
 - The AI Act’s accuracy and robustness requirements (**Article 15**) must be demonstrated as part of the clinical evaluation and post-market follow-up.

4. Risk Management:
 - The MDR risk management system (**Annex I**, Chapter I) and the AI Act risk management system (**Article 9**) must be aligned. Risks arising from continuous learning (e.g., model drift, bias amplification) must be addressed in both systems.

5. Technical Documentation:
 - The MDR technical documentation (**Annex II** and III) and the AI Act technical documentation (**Article 11** and **Annex IV**) can be combined to avoid duplication. Ensure the documentation covers both MDR and AI Act requirements, including data governance, risk management, and post-market monitoring.

6. Serious Incident Reporting:
 - Serious incidents must be reported under both the MDR (**Article 87**) and the AI Act (**Article 73**). The AI Act’s reporting obligations are additional to the MDR’s and focus on AI-specific risks (e.g., malfunctions due to continuous learning).

7. Transparency and Human Oversight:
 - The MDR’s instructions for use (**Article 2(14)**) must include information on the AI system’s limitations, performance metrics, and human oversight mechanisms, as required by **Article 13** AI Act.
 - The AI Act’s transparency obligations (**Article 13**) go further by requiring clear communication about the system’s AI nature, its decision-making process, and the role of human oversight.

8. Data Governance:
 - The AI Act’s data governance requirements (**Article 10**) must be integrated into the MDR’s clinical evaluation and risk management processes. Ensure datasets used for training, validation, and testing are representative, unbiased, and relevant to the intended use population.

---

### 6. Residual Uncertainty and Key Considerations
1. Continuous-Learning Behaviour:
 - The AI Act explicitly addresses continuous-learning models in **Article 15(4)**, requiring you to:
 - Eliminate or reduce risks of feedback loops (e.g., biased outputs influencing future inputs).
 - Implement mitigation measures to address model drift or performance degradation.
 - Document these measures in the technical documentation and risk management system.
 - The MDR does not explicitly address continuous learning, but the PMS and PMCF systems must monitor its impact on clinical performance and safety. You must update the clinical evaluation and risk management file as the model evolves.

2. Conformity Assessment Timing:
 - The AI Act’s general application date (2 August 2026) means its obligations for high-risk AI systems (including **Article 6(1)**) will apply from that date. However, the MDR has been in force since 26 May 2021, so its obligations are already applicable.
 - Ensure your conformity assessment under the MDR accounts for the AI Act requirements from 2 August 2026 onward. If your device is already on the market, you may need to update your technical documentation and QMS to comply with the AI Act.

3. Notified Body Involvement:
 - The notified body assessing your device under the MDR will also assess compliance with the AI Act’s high-risk requirements. Ensure the notified body has AI expertise or access to AI specialists to evaluate your system’s compliance.

4. Combined Declarations of Conformity:
 - Under **Article 47(3)** AI Act, you may draw up a single EU declaration of conformity covering both the MDR and AI Act requirements. This simplifies compliance but requires careful documentation to address both sets of obligations.

5. Post-Market Updates:
 - If your continuous-learning model undergoes substantial modifications (e.g., changes to the learning algorithm or training data), you must:
 - Assess whether the modification is likely to trigger a new conformity assessment under the MDR (**Article 10(9)** MDR).
 - Update the AI Act technical documentation and risk management system to reflect the changes.
 - Inform the notified body and competent authorities as required.

6. Case-by-Case Assessment for Continuous Learning:
 - The impact of continuous learning on compliance must be assessed case-by-case. Factors to consider include:
 - The degree of autonomy in the learning process (e.g., fully autonomous vs. human-supervised updates).
 - The risk of performance degradation (e.g., model drift, bias amplification).
 - The frequency and scope of updates (e.g., minor adjustments vs. major retraining).
 - Document your assessment methodology and mitigation measures in the technical documentation.

---

### Conclusion: Likely Outcome and Next Steps
1. Your AI system is a high-risk AI system under the AI Act via Route I (**Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**) because it is a Class IIb medical device subject to third-party conformity assessment under the MDR.
2. You must comply with both the MDR and AI Act obligations, with the MDR conformity assessment procedure serving as the primary route for demonstrating compliance. The AI Act requirements are integrated into this assessment.
3. Continuous-learning behaviour is likely to trigger additional obligations under both regulations, particularly for risk management, post-market monitoring, and transparency. You must:
 - Implement measures to mitigate feedback loops and model drift.
 - Establish a robust post-market surveillance system to monitor performance and safety.
 - Update technical documentation, risk management, and clinical evaluation as the model evolves.
4. Key next steps:
 - Integrate AI Act requirements into your MDR conformity assessment and QMS.
 - Align your risk management, clinical evaluation, and post-market monitoring systems with both regulations.
 - Document your approach to continuous learning, including mitigation measures and performance monitoring.
 - Engage a notified body with AI expertise to assess compliance with both the MDR and AI Act.
 - Prepare for the AI Act’s general application date (2 August 2026) by ensuring your device complies with its requirements by that time.

flat.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment with partial retrieval support. Treat conclusions below as tentative and case-specific unless directly quoted from the retrieved provisions.

### 1. Actor Status
#### Under the EU AI Act
- You are the provider of the AI system under **Article 3(3)** of the AI Act because you developed the system and placed it on the market under your own name or trademark.
- The continuous-learning behaviour does not alter this status; the AI Act’s definition of "AI system" explicitly includes systems that exhibit adaptiveness after deployment.

#### Under the MDR 2017/745
- You are the manufacturer of the medical device under **Article 2(30)** of the MDR, as you placed the device on the market under your own name.

---

### 2. Classification Route
#### Under the EU AI Act
- Your AI system is high-risk under **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**, Section A (MDR 2017/745) because:
 1. It is a safety component of a product (the Class IIb medical device) covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in **Annex I**.
 2. The product (Class IIb medical device) is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment under the MDR (see **Article 52(4)** MDR, which mandates notified body involvement for Class IIb devices).
- Route I (**Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**) applies and is mandatory for medical devices in Class IIa, IIb, or III. No derogation applies.
- Route II (**Article 6(2)** + **Annex III**) is irrelevant unless your system also performs a biometric, essential-service, or other **Annex III** function (e.g., profiling for employment). The AI Act’s **Annex III** does not include a standalone "medical diagnosis" category.

#### Under the MDR 2017/745
- Your software is classified as Class IIb under **Annex VIII**, Chapter III, Rule 11 (software for diagnostic/therapeutic decisions with serious impact on health). This classification is binding unless reclassified by the Commission under **Article 51(3)** MDR.

---

### 3. Trigger Event
- Placing the device on the market (MDR) and placing the AI system on the market (AI Act) are the trigger events. For the AI Act, this includes putting the system into service under your own name, even if only internally (see **Article 3(3)**).
- The continuous-learning behaviour is likely to trigger additional obligations under both regulations, as it is likely to constitute a dynamic change to the device/system post-deployment.

---

### 4. Legal Effects: Obligation Clusters
#### A. EU AI Act Obligations for High-Risk AI Systems
##### Post-Market Monitoring (PMM) for Continuous Learning
- **Article 72(1)** and (2) require you to establish a post-market monitoring system proportionate to the risks of your high-risk AI system. This system must:
 - Actively and systematically collect, document, and analyse relevant data on performance throughout the system’s lifetime, including data from deployers or other sources.
 - Enable evaluation of continuous compliance with Chapter III, Section 2 requirements (e.g., risk management, data governance).
 - Include analysis of interactions with other AI systems (e.g., if your model integrates with other software).
 - Exclude sensitive operational data of deployers that are law enforcement authorities.
- **Article 72(3)** mandates a post-market monitoring plan, which must be part of the technical documentation (**Annex IV**). The Commission will adopt a template for this plan by 2 February 2026 (

> 3.   The post-market monitoring system shall be based on a post-market monitoring plan. The post-market monitoring plan shall be part of the technical documentation referred to in Annex IV. The Commission shall adopt an implementing act laying down detailed provisions establishing a template for the post-market monitoring plan and the list of elements to be included in the plan by 2 February 2026. That implementing act shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2).

).
- **Article 72(4)** allows integration of the AI Act’s PMM system into your existing MDR post-market surveillance (PMS) system (see **Article 83** MDR), provided it achieves an equivalent level of protection. This avoids duplication but requires:
 - Alignment of data collection (e.g., clinical performance data under MDR must also inform AI Act compliance).
 - Documentation of how the integrated system meets both regulations’ requirements.

##### Risk Management for Continuous Learning
- **Article 9(2)**(c) requires your risk management system to evaluate risks arising from post-market monitoring data, including those from continuous learning. This means:
 - You must continuously assess whether the model’s adaptations introduce new risks (e.g., bias, performance drift).
 - **Article 9(2)**(d) mandates mitigation measures for identified risks, which may include:
 - Retraining protocols with human oversight.
 - Performance thresholds for model updates.
 - Alert mechanisms for deployers if the model deviates from expected behaviour.

##### Technical Documentation and Transparency
- **Annex IV** requires technical documentation that includes:
 - A description of the AI system’s design and development, including continuous-learning mechanisms (e.g., retraining is likely to trigger, data sources, validation methods).
 - Risk management measures for continuous learning (see above).
 - Post-market monitoring plan (see **Article 72(3)**).
- **Article 13** requires transparency for deployers, including:
 - Clear instructions for use, including limitations of continuous-learning behaviour (e.g., scenarios where the model may underperform).
 - Information on performance monitoring and how deployers can report issues.

##### Serious Incident Reporting
- **Article 73(1)** obliges you to report serious incidents (e.g., death, serious health deterioration, or fundamental rights violations) to the market surveillance authorities of the Member State where the incident occurred.
 - Timelines:
 - Immediately (but no later than 15 days) after establishing a causal link between the AI system and the incident.
 - 2 days for widespread infringements or serious incidents as defined in **Article 3(49)**(b).
 - 10 days for incidents resulting in death.
 - **Article 73(5)** allows submission of an initial incomplete report followed by a complete report if necessary.
- **Article 73(11)** requires national authorities to notify the Commission of serious incidents.

##### Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA)
- **Article 27** requires a fundamental rights impact assessment if your system is used in a context that could affect fundamental rights (e.g., healthcare decisions). This must include:
 - An assessment of risks to fundamental rights (e.g., non-discrimination, privacy, autonomy).
 - Mitigation measures for identified risks (e.g., bias testing, explainability tools).
 - Consultation with stakeholders (e.g., patient groups, clinicians) where relevant.

---

#### B. MDR 2017/745 Obligations for Class IIb Devices
##### Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) and Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF)
- **Article 83** requires a PMS system proportionate to the risk class (Class IIb) and type of device. This system must:
 - Actively gather, record, and analyse relevant data on quality, performance, and safety throughout the device’s lifetime.
 - Use the data to update the benefit-risk determination, risk management, clinical evaluation, and instructions for use.
 - Detect and report trends (e.g., performance drift due to continuous learning) under **Article 88**.
- **Annex III** specifies the PMS plan must include:
 - Proactive collection of serious incidents, non-serious incidents, trend reports, user feedback, and publicly available information about similar devices.
 - Methods and processes to assess collected data, including thresholds for reassessing benefit-risk.
 - A PMCF plan (see **Annex XIV**, Part B) or justification for its non-applicability.
- **Annex XIV**, Part B requires PMCF to:
 - Confirm safety and performance throughout the device’s expected lifetime.
 - Identify previously unknown side-effects and emerging risks (e.g., from continuous learning).
 - Ensure continued acceptability of the benefit-risk ratio.
 - The PMCF plan must include:
 - Methods for proactive data collection (e.g., registries, clinical studies).
 - Specific objectives (e.g., monitoring model performance in real-world settings).
 - Time schedules for analysis and reporting.

##### Notified Body Involvement
- **Annex IX**, Chapter I, point 3.2 requires you to provide the notified body with:
 - Documentation on findings and conclusions from PMS, including PMCF data and vigilance reports (

> documentation on any findings and conclusions resulting from the application of the post-market surveillance plan, including the PMCF plan, for a representative sample of devices, and of the provisions on vigilance set out in Articles 87 to 92,

).
 - This includes data from continuous learning (e.g., performance metrics, retraining logs).
- **Annex IX**, Chapter II, point 4.6 requires the notified body to verify:
 - The adequacy of clinical evidence and clinical evaluation, including benefit-risk determination.
 - The PMCF plan (if applicable) to ensure it addresses emerging risks from continuous learning.
- **Annex IX**, Chapter II, point 5.1 (for Class IIb devices) may trigger expert panel review if:
 - There is a significantly increased rate of serious incidents (e.g., due to model drift).
 - The notified body’s clinical evaluation assessment report raises concerns about benefit-risk determination or PMCF plan adequacy.

##### Vigilance and Trend Reporting
- **Article 87** requires reporting of serious incidents to the competent authority within:
 - 15 days for incidents.
 - 10 days for incidents resulting in death.
- **Article 88** requires trend reporting if there is a statistically significant increase in the frequency or severity of incidents (e.g., due to continuous learning).

##### Technical Documentation and Updates
- **Annex II** and III require technical documentation that includes:
 - Design and manufacturing information, including continuous-learning mechanisms (e.g., retraining protocols, data sources).
 - Risk management file (see **Annex I**, Section 3), updated with post-market data, including risks from continuous learning.
 - Clinical evaluation report (see **Article 61**), updated with PMCF data and benefit-risk reassessments.

---

### 5. Cross-Regulatory Chain
| Obligation | MDR 2017/745 | EU AI Act | Cross-Regulatory Dependency |
|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Post-market monitoring | **Article 83**, **Annex III** | **Article 72** | **Article 72(4)** AI Act allows integration of AI Act PMM into MDR PMS if equivalent protection is achieved. |
| Risk management | **Annex I**, Section 3 | **Article 9** | MDR risk management must include AI Act requirements (e.g., evaluation of post-market data for continuous learning). |
| Technical documentation | **Annex II**, III | **Annex IV** | MDR technical documentation must include AI Act **Annex IV** elements (e.g., PMM plan, continuous-learning mechanisms). |
| Serious incident reporting| **Article 87** | **Article 73** | **Article 73(1)** AI Act requires reporting to MDR competent authorities. |
| Clinical evaluation | **Article 61**, **Annex XIV** | **Article 10(2)**(f) (data governance) | MDR clinical evaluation must address AI Act data quality requirements (e.g., bias, representativeness). |
| Transparency | **Article 10(11)** (IFU) | **Article 13** | MDR instructions for use must include AI Act transparency requirements (e.g., limitations of continuous learning). |

---

### 6. Residual Uncertainty
1. Continuous-Learning Mechanisms:
 - The AI Act does not explicitly define "continuous learning" or prescribe specific controls. You must interpret the risk management and PMM requirements to address:
 - Retraining is likely to trigger (e.g., performance degradation, new data availability).
 - Validation methods for model updates (e.g., holdout datasets, clinical validation).
 - Human oversight for model adaptations (e.g., clinician review of outputs).
 - MDCG guidance (not in the retrieved context) may provide further clarification on acceptable practices.

2. Integration of MDR and AI Act Systems:
 - **Article 72(4)** AI Act allows integration of PMM systems, but the Commission’s template (due February 2026) will clarify how to document equivalence. Until then, you must justify your approach in technical documentation.

3. Notified Body and Expert Panel Scrutiny:
 - The notified body may request additional evidence to demonstrate that continuous learning does not compromise safety or performance. Expert panel review (**Annex IX**, Chapter II, point 5.1) could be triggered if there are serious incident trends or benefit-risk concerns.

4. Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA):
 - The scope of the FRIA (**Article 27** AI Act) for medical devices is unclear. You must assess whether your system’s use (e.g., diagnostic decisions) affects fundamental rights (e.g., non-discrimination, autonomy) and document mitigation measures.

---

### 7. Likely Outcome and Recommendations
#### Likely Classification and Obligations
- Your AI system is high-risk under the AI Act (Route I) and your device is Class IIb under the MDR.
- You must comply with both regulations’ post-market obligations, with the option to integrate systems under **Article 72(4)** AI Act.
- Continuous learning is permitted but heavily regulated, requiring:
 - Proactive monitoring of model performance and risks.
 - Documented retraining protocols with human oversight.
 - Regular updates to risk management, clinical evaluation, and technical documentation.

#### Recommendations
1. Integrate Post-Market Systems:
 - Align your MDR PMS system with the AI Act PMM system to avoid duplication. Document how the integrated system meets both regulations’ requirements.

2. Address Continuous Learning in Risk Management:
 - Define retraining is likely to trigger (e.g., performance thresholds, new data availability).
 - Implement validation methods for model updates (e.g., clinical validation, bias testing).
 - Establish human oversight for model adaptations (e.g., clinician review of outputs).

3. Prepare for Notified Body Scrutiny:
 - Ensure your technical documentation (MDR **Annex II**/III and AI Act **Annex IV**) includes:
 - Continuous-learning mechanisms (e.g., retraining protocols, data sources).
 - Risk management measures for model adaptations.
 - PMCF plan (if applicable) to monitor real-world performance.

4. Conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA):
 - Assess risks to fundamental rights (e.g., non-discrimination, autonomy) and document mitigation measures (e.g., bias testing, explainability tools).

5. Monitor Regulatory Developments:
 - Await the Commission’s PMM plan template (due February 2026) for guidance on documenting equivalence between MDR and AI Act systems.
 - Monitor MDCG guidance on continuous-learning models in medical devices.

results.label: AI Act high-risk qualification — triage chatbot (Annex III vs Article 6(1))

full.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination.

### 1. Actor Status
The company that develops and markets the AI chatbot is the provider under the EU AI Act (**Article 3(3)**), as it places the AI system on the market under its own name or trademark. Under the MDR 2017/745, the same company is the manufacturer (**Article 2(30)**), as it determines the intended medical purpose of the software.

---

### 2. Classification Route
The AI chatbot must be analysed under **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I** (Route I) first, as it is a medical device software (MDSW) with a medical purpose (symptom triage and emergency care recommendation). Only if Route I does not apply should **Annex III** (Route II) be considered.

#### Route I (Article 6(1) + Annex I)
- Condition (a): The AI system is itself a product (software) covered by MDR 2017/745, which is listed in **Annex I**, Section A(11) of the AI Act.
- Condition (b): The product (AI chatbot) is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment under the MDR. Since the chatbot triages symptoms and recommends emergency care, it likely falls under MDR Class IIa or higher (see **Annex VIII**, Rule 11 of the MDR, which classifies MDSW providing information for diagnostic or therapeutic decisions as Class IIa or higher). For Class IIa, IIb, or III devices, third-party conformity assessment is mandatory (**Article 52** MDR).

Conclusion: The AI chatbot satisfies both conditions of **Article 6(1)** and is therefore a high-risk AI system under Route I. No derogation applies to Route I.

#### Route II (Annex III)
Even if Route I were not applicable, **Annex III**, point 5(d) of the AI Act could apply:
> *"AI systems intended to evaluate and classify emergency calls by natural persons or to be used to dispatch, or to establish priority in the dispatching of, emergency first response services, including by police, firefighters and medical aid, as well as of emergency healthcare patient triage systems."*

The chatbot’s function (triage and recommendation for emergency care) aligns with emergency healthcare patient triage systems, which are explicitly listed in **Annex III**, point 5(d). However, Route I takes precedence for medical devices, so this is a secondary consideration.

---

### 3. Trigger Event
The placing on the market of the AI chatbot (first making it available in the EU) is likely to trigger the high-risk obligations under the AI Act (**Article 2(1)**). Under the MDR, the same event is likely to trigger the medical device conformity assessment and post-market surveillance obligations (**Article 10** MDR).

---

### 4. Legal Effects
#### AI Act Obligations (High-Risk AI System)
As a high-risk AI system under **Article 6(1)**, the provider must comply with Chapter III, Section 2 of the AI Act, including:
- Risk management system (**Article 9**): Continuous iterative process to identify and mitigate risks.
- Data governance (**Article 10**): Ensuring training, validation, and testing datasets are relevant, representative, and free of errors/bias.
- Technical documentation (**Article 11**): Detailed records demonstrating compliance with high-risk requirements.
- Transparency and human oversight (**Article 13**-14): Instructions for use, logging capabilities, and human oversight mechanisms.
- Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity (**Article 15**): Ensuring the system performs consistently and securely.
- Conformity assessment (**Article 43(3)**): The provider must follow the MDR conformity assessment procedure, with the AI Act requirements integrated into that assessment. For Class IIa devices, this typically involves **Annex XI** (Part A) of the MDR (conformity assessment based on production quality assurance).
- Post-market monitoring (**Article 72**): Continuous collection and review of data on the AI system’s performance and risks.

#### MDR Obligations (Medical Device)
As a Class IIa medical device, the manufacturer must:
- Conduct a conformity assessment (**Article 52** MDR), typically involving a notified body for production quality assurance (**Annex XI**, Part A MDR).
- Establish a quality management system (**Article 10(9)** MDR).
- Prepare technical documentation (**Annex II** MDR), including clinical evaluation (**Annex XIV** MDR).
- Implement post-market surveillance (PMS) (**Article 83** MDR) and post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF) if applicable (**Annex XIV**, Part B MDR).
- Report serious incidents and field safety corrective actions (FSCAs) (**Article 87** MDR).

#### Cross-Regulatory Chain
- The MDR conformity assessment (**Article 52** MDR) serves as the primary conformity assessment for the AI Act (**Article 43(3)** AI Act), with the AI Act’s high-risk requirements (Chapter III, Section 2) integrated into the MDR process.
- The clinical evaluation under the MDR (**Annex XIV** MDR) supports the performance and safety requirements of the AI Act (**Article 15** AI Act).
- Post-market surveillance (PMS) under the MDR (**Article 83** MDR) aligns with the post-market monitoring requirements of the AI Act (**Article 72** AI Act), ensuring continuous oversight of the AI system’s performance and risks.

---

### 5. Residual Uncertainty
- MDR Classification: The exact MDR class of the chatbot depends on its specific intended purpose and risk level. If the chatbot’s recommendations could lead to delayed or incorrect emergency care, it may be classified as Class IIb (higher risk). A case-by-case assessment is required, as the MDR does not provide a definitive classification for all triage software.
- **Annex III** Applicability: While the chatbot’s function aligns with **Annex III**, point 5(d), this route is secondary to Route I for medical devices. However, if the chatbot were not classified as a medical device (e.g., if its intended purpose were non-medical), **Annex III**, point 5(d) would become the primary route for high-risk classification.
- Derogation under **Article 6(3)** AI Act: If the chatbot were analysed under **Annex III**, the provider could argue that it performs a narrow procedural task (e.g., symptom logging without influencing clinical decisions) or improves the result of a previously completed human activity (e.g., assisting a clinician in triage). However, profiling of natural persons (e.g., patient risk stratification) would always remain high-risk (**Article 6(3)**, subparagraph 3 AI Act).

---

### Final Conclusion
The AI chatbot is high-risk under the EU AI Act, primarily via **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I** (Route I), because:
1. It is a medical device software (MDSW) under the MDR 2017/745, which is listed in **Annex I** of the AI Act.
2. It is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment under the MDR (likely Class IIa or higher).
3. Route I takes precedence over **Annex III** for medical devices.

The provider must comply with the high-risk obligations of the AI Act (Chapter III, Section 2) and the MDR requirements for Class IIa/IIb medical devices, with the MDR conformity assessment serving as the primary procedure for both regulations.

flat.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment with partial retrieval support. Treat conclusions below as tentative and case-specific unless directly quoted from the retrieved provisions.

### 1. Actor Status
The company that develops and markets the AI chatbot is the provider under the EU AI Act, as defined in **Article 3(3)**:
> ‘provider’ means a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body that develops an AI system or a general-purpose AI model or that has an AI system or a general-purpose AI model developed and places it on the market or puts the AI system into service under its own name or trademark, whether for payment or free of charge.

Since the company places the AI system on the market under its own name, it qualifies as a provider from inception.

---

### 2. Classification Route
The AI Act establishes two routes for high-risk classification, which must be analysed in order:

#### Route I: Article 6(1) + Annex I (Product Route)
Under **Article 6(1)**, an AI system is high-risk if:
1. It is a safety component of a product, or itself a product, covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in **Annex I**; and
2. The product (or the AI system as a product) is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment under that legislation.

**Annex I**, Section A, point 11 explicitly lists Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR) as a relevant Union harmonisation legislation.

Key question: Is the AI chatbot a medical device under the MDR?
- The formal definition of ‘medical device’ in **Article 2** MDR includes software intended for medical purposes, such as diagnosis, monitoring, or treatment.
- The chatbot triages patient symptoms and recommends whether to seek emergency care, which qualifies as a diagnostic or decision-support function with a medical purpose.
- Under **Annex VIII**, Chapter III, point 6.3 MDR (Rule 11), software intended to provide information for diagnostic or therapeutic decisions is classified as Class IIa, unless the decision could cause death, irreversible deterioration, or serious deterioration of health, in which case it is Class III or IIb.
 - The chatbot’s output (recommending emergency care) could influence decisions with serious health consequences, likely placing it in Class IIa or higher.
 - Class IIa, IIb, and III devices require third-party conformity assessment under the MDR.

Conclusion for Route I:
- The AI chatbot is software with a medical purpose, making it a medical device under the MDR.
- As a Class IIa (or higher) device, it is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment.
- Therefore, both conditions of **Article 6(1)** are satisfied: the AI system is a product covered by **Annex I** (MDR), and third-party conformity assessment is required.
- Route I applies, and the system is high-risk under **Article 6(1)**.

#### Route II: Article 6(2) + Annex III (Use-Case Route)
Under **Article 6(2)**, AI systems listed in **Annex III** are also high-risk. However:
- **Annex III** does not include a standalone ‘medical diagnosis’ or ‘triage’ category.
- The closest relevant category is **Annex III**, point 5(a), which covers AI systems used by public authorities to evaluate eligibility for essential public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare services.
 - This applies only if the chatbot is used by or on behalf of public authorities to determine eligibility for healthcare services (e.g., emergency care access).
 - If the chatbot is used by private entities (e.g., hospitals, telemedicine platforms) or individuals, this category does not apply.
- **Annex III**, point 5(d) covers AI systems for emergency healthcare patient triage, but this is limited to systems used to dispatch or establish priority in dispatching emergency first response services (e.g., ambulance dispatch).
 - A symptom-triage chatbot that recommends whether to seek emergency care does not directly dispatch or prioritise emergency services, so this category is unlikely to apply.

Derogation under **Article 6(3)**:
Even if the chatbot fell under **Annex III**, **Article 6(3)** allows a derogation if the system:
- Does not pose a significant risk of harm to health, safety, or fundamental rights; and
- Does not materially influence decision-making (e.g., performs a narrow procedural task or improves a human activity without replacing it).

However:
- The chatbot’s output directly influences medical decisions (whether to seek emergency care), which materially affects patient outcomes.
- It is unlikely to qualify for the derogation, as it does not meet the conditions in **Article 6(3)**(a)–(d).

Conclusion for Route II:
- The chatbot does not clearly fall under any **Annex III** category.
- Even if it did, the derogation in **Article 6(3)** is unlikely to apply due to its influence on medical decisions.
- Route II does not apply.

---

### 3. Trigger Event
The trigger event for high-risk obligations is:
- Placing the AI system on the market (first making available in the EU) or putting it into service (making it ready for use for its intended purpose), as defined in **Article 2** MDR and **Article 3** AI Act.

---

### 4. Legal Effects
Since the chatbot is high-risk under **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I**, the following obligations apply to the provider:

#### AI Act Obligations (Binding)
1. Risk Management System (**Article 9**):
 - Establish, implement, document, and maintain a continuous risk management system throughout the AI system’s lifecycle.
2. Data and Data Governance (**Article 10**):
 - Ensure training, validation, and testing datasets are relevant, representative, free of errors, and complete.
 - Address biases and ensure data quality.
3. Technical Documentation (**Article 11**):
 - Prepare and maintain technical documentation demonstrating compliance with the AI Act.
4. Record-Keeping (**Article 12**):
 - Enable automatic recording of events (logs) to ensure traceability.
5. Transparency and Provision of Information (**Article 13**):
 - Ensure the system is transparent and provides instructions for use to deployers.
6. Human Oversight (**Article 14**):
 - Design the system to enable effective human oversight (e.g., allowing healthcare professionals to review recommendations).
7. Accuracy, Robustness, and Cybersecurity (**Article 15**):
 - Ensure the system is accurate, robust, and secure against cyber threats.
8. Conformity Assessment (**Article 43**):
 - Undergo third-party conformity assessment by a notified body (since the system is a medical device under the MDR, the MDR conformity assessment procedure applies).
 - The MDR conformity assessment (e.g., **Annex XI**, Part A for Class IIa devices) prevails for high-risk AI systems that are also medical devices (see interpretive guidance in the context).
9. Registration (**Article 49**):
 - Register the system in the EU database for high-risk AI systems.
10. Post-Market Monitoring (**Article 72**):
 - Implement a post-market monitoring system to track performance and risks.
11. Serious Incident Reporting (**Article 73**):
 - Report serious incidents to market surveillance authorities, in addition to MDR reporting obligations.

#### MDR Obligations (Binding)
Since the chatbot is a Class IIa (or higher) medical device, the following MDR obligations apply:
1. Conformity Assessment (**Annex XI**, Part A):
 - For Class IIa devices, the manufacturer must:
 - Lodge an application for assessment of its quality management system with a notified body (**Annex XI**, Part A, point 6).
 - Submit technical documentation (**Annexes II** and III MDR).
 - Ensure compliance with the type described in the EU type-examination certificate (if applicable).
2. Technical Documentation (**Annex II** and III MDR):
 - Prepare and maintain technical documentation demonstrating compliance with MDR requirements.
3. Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) (**Article 83**–86 MDR, **Annex III** MDR):
 - Establish a PMS system to actively collect and analyse data on the device’s performance and safety.
 - Prepare a PMS plan (**Annex III** MDR) and, for Class IIa devices, a post-market surveillance report (**Article 85** MDR).
4. Clinical Evaluation (**Article 61** MDR):
 - Conduct a clinical evaluation to demonstrate conformity with safety and performance requirements.
 - For Class IIa devices, this may involve literature reviews, clinical investigations, or equivalence assessments (**Article 61(3)** MDR).
5. Trend Reporting (**Article 88** MDR):
 - Report statistically significant increases in the frequency or severity of incidents that could impact the benefit-risk analysis.
6. UDI and Registration (**Article 27** and 29 MDR):
 - Assign a Unique Device Identifier (UDI) and register the device in EUDAMED.

---

### 5. Cross-Regulatory Chain
The AI Act and MDR obligations interact as follows:
1. Conformity Assessment:
 - The MDR conformity assessment procedure (e.g., **Annex XI**, Part A for Class IIa) applies to the AI system, as it is a medical device.
 - The AI Act does not impose a separate conformity assessment for high-risk AI systems that are also medical devices (see interpretive guidance in the context).
2. Clinical Evidence:
 - The MDR requires clinical evaluation to demonstrate safety and performance.
 - The AI Act requires robust data governance (**Article 10**) and accuracy/robustness (**Article 15**), which complement the MDR’s clinical evidence requirements.
3. Post-Market Obligations:
 - The MDR’s PMS system (**Article 83**–86) and AI Act’s post-market monitoring (**Article 72**) overlap.
 - The MDR’s trend reporting (**Article 88**) and AI Act’s serious incident reporting (**Article 73**) must be coordinated to avoid duplication.
4. Transparency and Human Oversight:
 - The AI Act’s transparency requirements (**Article 13**) and human oversight (**Article 14**) align with the MDR’s instructions for use (**Annex I**, Chapter III, point 23 MDR) and risk management (**Annex I**, Chapter I, point 3 MDR).

---

### 6. Residual Uncertainty
1. MDR Classification:
 - The exact MDR class (IIa, IIb, or III) depends on the severity of the decisions the chatbot influences.
 - If the chatbot’s recommendations could lead to death or irreversible deterioration of health, it may be Class III (requiring stricter conformity assessment).
 - If the recommendations could lead to serious deterioration or surgical intervention, it may be Class IIb.
 - Otherwise, it is Class IIa.
 - Case-by-case assessment is required to determine the exact class.

2. **Annex III** Applicability:
 - If the chatbot is used by public authorities to determine eligibility for healthcare services, it may fall under **Annex III**, point 5(a).
 - However, this is unlikely unless the chatbot is integrated into a public healthcare system’s eligibility assessment process.

3. Derogation under **Article 6(3)**:
 - If the chatbot is solely used to improve a previously completed human activity (e.g., assisting a doctor in triage) without replacing human judgment, it might qualify for the derogation.
 - However, if the chatbot influences or replaces human decision-making (e.g., directly recommending emergency care without human review), the derogation does not apply.

---

### Final Conclusion
The AI chatbot is high-risk under the EU AI Act, and the legal basis is **Article 6(1)** + **Annex I** (Route I). This is because:
1. The chatbot is software with a medical purpose, making it a medical device under the MDR.
2. As a Class IIa (or higher) medical device, it is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment under the MDR.
3. Both conditions of **Article 6(1)** are satisfied: the AI system is a product covered by **Annex I** (MDR), and third-party conformity assessment is required.
4. Route II (**Annex III**) does not apply, as the chatbot does not clearly fall under any **Annex III** category, and the derogation in **Article 6(3)** is unlikely to apply.

Key obligations:
- The provider must comply with both the AI Act (high-risk obligations) and the MDR (medical device obligations).
- The MDR conformity assessment procedure (e.g., **Annex XI**, Part A for Class IIa) applies, and the AI Act does not impose a separate conformity assessment.
- The provider must ensure clinical evaluation (MDR), data governance (AI Act), transparency (AI Act), and post-market monitoring (both regulations).

results.label: AI Act GPAI model provider — systemic-risk obligations (Article 53 + 55)

full.answer:
Under the EU AI Act, a provider of a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk is subject to a comprehensive set of obligations designed to ensure transparency, risk mitigation, and regulatory compliance. These obligations are primarily governed by Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models), particularly **Article 55**, which builds upon the baseline obligations for all general-purpose AI models (**Article 53**) and the requirements for authorised representatives (**Article 54**). Below is a structured analysis of the provider’s obligations, grounded exclusively in the EU AI Act provisions provided in the regulatory context.

---

### 1. Baseline Obligations for All General-Purpose AI Models (Article 53)
Before addressing systemic-risk-specific obligations, the provider must comply with the baseline obligations for all general-purpose AI models, as set out in **Article 53(1)**. These include:
- Technical Documentation (**Article 53(1)**(a)):
 The provider must draw up and keep up-to-date technical documentation of the model, including its training and testing process and evaluation results. This documentation must contain, at a minimum, the information specified in **Annex XI** and must be made available to the AI Office and national competent authorities upon request.

> draw up and keep up-to-date the technical documentation of the model, including its training and testing process and the results of its evaluation, which shall contain, at a minimum, the information set out in Annex XI for the purpose of providing it, upon request, to the AI Office and the national competent authorities;

- Information for Downstream Providers (**Article 53(1)**(b)):
 The provider must draw up, keep up-to-date, and make available information and documentation to enable providers of AI systems that integrate the general-purpose AI model to understand its capabilities and limitations and comply with their own obligations under the AI Act. This must include, at a minimum, the elements set out in **Annex XII**.
 The documentation must be sufficient to enable downstream providers to comply with their obligations (e.g., risk management, transparency, and conformity assessment for high-risk AI systems).

> draw up, keep up-to-date and make available information and documentation to providers of AI systems who intend to integrate the general-purpose AI model into their AI systems. Without prejudice to the need to observe and protect intellectual property rights and confidential business information or trade secrets in accordance with Union and national law, the information and documentation shall:

- Copyright Compliance (**Article 53(1)**(c)):
 The provider must implement a policy to comply with Union law on copyright and related rights, including mechanisms to identify and comply with reservations of rights expressed under **Article 4(3)** of Directive (EU) 2019/790 (e.g., opt-outs for text and data mining).

> put in place a policy to comply with Union law on copyright and related rights, and in particular to identify and comply with, including through state-of-the-art technologies, a reservation of rights expressed pursuant to Article 4(3) of Directive (EU) 2019/790;

- Public Summary of Training Data (**Article 53(1)**(d)):
 The provider must draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary of the content used for training the model, following a template provided by the AI Office.

> draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary about the content used for training of the general-purpose AI model, according to a template provided by the AI Office.

- Exceptions for Open-Source Models (**Article 53(2)**):
 The obligations under **Article 53(1)**(a) and (b) do not apply to providers of general-purpose AI models released under a free and open-source licence where:
 - The model’s parameters (including weights, architecture, and usage information) are publicly available, and
 - The model does not present systemic risks.

> 2.   The obligations set out in paragraph 1, points (a) and (b), shall not apply to providers of AI models that are released under a free and open-source licence that allows for the access, usage, modification, and distribution of the model, and whose parameters, including the weights, the information on the model architecture, and the information on model usage, are made publicly available. This exception shall not apply to general-purpose AI models with systemic risks.

---

### 2. Systemic-Risk-Specific Obligations (Article 55)
In addition to the baseline obligations, providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk must comply with enhanced requirements under **Article 55(1)**. These obligations are designed to address the higher potential for widespread harm associated with such models. The key obligations are:

- Model Evaluation and Adversarial Testing (**Article 55(1)**(a)):
 The provider must perform model evaluation in accordance with standardised protocols and tools reflecting the state of the art, including:
 - Adversarial testing to identify and mitigate systemic risks.
 - Documentation of the testing process and results.

> perform model evaluation in accordance with standardised protocols and tools reflecting the state of the art, including conducting and documenting adversarial testing of the model with a view to identifying and mitigating systemic risks;

- Systemic Risk Assessment and Mitigation (**Article 55(1)**(b)):
 The provider must assess and mitigate possible systemic risks at Union level, including their sources, that may arise from:
 - The development of the model,
 - Its placing on the market, or
 - Its use.
 This assessment must be continuous and proactive, addressing risks such as bias propagation, misinformation, or threats to public health and safety.

> assess and mitigate possible systemic risks at Union level, including their sources, that may stem from the development, the placing on the market, or the use of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk;

- Incident Reporting (**Article 55(1)**(c)):
 The provider must:
 - Keep track of and document relevant information about serious incidents (as defined in **Article 3(44)**) and possible corrective measures.
 - Report such incidents without undue delay to the AI Office and, where appropriate, to national competent authorities.

> keep track of, document, and report, without undue delay, to the AI Office and, as appropriate, to national competent authorities, relevant information about serious incidents and possible corrective measures to address them;

- Cybersecurity (**Article 55(1)**(d)):
 The provider must ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity protection for:
 - The general-purpose AI model with systemic risk, and
 - The physical infrastructure supporting the model.
 This includes measures to prevent unauthorised access, data breaches, or malicious exploitation of the model.

> ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity protection for the general-purpose AI model with systemic risk and the physical infrastructure of the model.

---

### 3. Authorised Representative Obligations (Article 54)
If the provider is established outside the EU, it must appoint an authorised representative in the Union before placing the model on the market. The authorised representative’s obligations, as set out in **Article 54(3)**, include:
- Verification of Compliance (**Article 54(3)**(a)):
 The authorised representative must verify that the provider has:
 - Drawn up the technical documentation (**Annex XI**), and
 - Fulfilled all obligations under **Article 53** and, where applicable, **Article 55**.

> verify that the technical documentation specified in Annex XI has been drawn up and all obligations referred to in Article 53 and, where applicable, Article 55 have been fulfilled by the provider;

- Documentation Retention (**Article 54(3)**(b)):
 The authorised representative must keep a copy of the technical documentation and the provider’s contact details for 10 years after the model is placed on the market, making it available to the AI Office and national competent authorities upon request.

> keep a copy of the technical documentation specified in Annex XI at the disposal of the AI Office and national competent authorities, for a period of 10 years after the general-purpose AI model has been placed on the market, and the contact details of the provider that appointed the authorised representative;

- Information Provision (**Article 54(3)**(c)):
 The authorised representative must provide the AI Office with all necessary information and documentation to demonstrate compliance with the obligations in Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models).

> provide the AI Office, upon a reasoned request, with all the information and documentation, including that referred to in point (b), necessary to demonstrate compliance with the obligations in this Chapter;

- Cooperation with Authorities (**Article 54(3)**(d)):
 The authorised representative must cooperate with the AI Office and national competent authorities in any actions related to the model, including when the model is integrated into AI systems placed on the market or put into service in the EU.

> cooperate with the AI Office and competent authorities, upon a reasoned request, in any action they take in relation to the general-purpose AI model, including when the model is integrated into AI systems placed on the market or put into service in the Union.

- Mandate Termination (**Article 54(5)**):
 The authorised representative must terminate the mandate if it considers or has reason to consider that the provider is acting contrary to its obligations under the AI Act. It must also immediately inform the AI Office of the termination and the reasons for it.

> 5.   The authorised representative shall terminate the mandate if it considers or has reason to consider the provider to be acting contrary to its obligations pursuant to this Regulation. In such a case, it shall also immediately inform the AI Office about the termination of the mandate and the reasons therefor.

- Exception for Open-Source Models (**Article 54(6)**):
 The obligation to appoint an authorised representative does not apply to providers of general-purpose AI models released under a free and open-source licence where:
