full.answer:
### Applicability of the EU AI Act to High-Risk AI Systems Listed in Annex III

#### 1. Prohibited AI Practices (Article 5) – Already Applicable
The prohibitions set out in **Article 5** of the AI Act apply from 2 February 2025 (

> Chapters I and II shall apply from 2 February 2025;

).
This means that as of this date, your high-risk AI system must not engage in any of the prohibited practices listed in **Article 5**, such as:
- Subliminal or manipulative techniques (**Article 5(1)**(a)),
- Exploitation of vulnerabilities (**Article 5(1)**(b)),
- Social scoring (**Article 5(1)**(c)),
- Untargeted scraping of facial images (**Article 5(1)**(e)),
- Emotion recognition in workplace/education (**Article 5(1)**(f)),
- Biometric categorisation based on sensitive attributes (**Article 5(1)**(g)),
- Unauthorised real-time remote biometric identification (**Article 5(1)**(h)).

#### 2. High-Risk Obligations (Chapter III, Annex III) – Not Yet Applicable
The general application of the AI Act, including high-risk obligations (e.g., conformity assessment, risk management, technical documentation, post-market monitoring), applies from 2 August 2026 (

> It shall apply from 2 August 2026.

).
However, **Article 6(1)** high-risk classification (for AI systems as safety components of products under Union harmonisation legislation, such as MDR/IVDR) applies only from 2 August 2027 (

> Article 6(1) and the corresponding obligations in this Regulation shall apply from 2 August 2027.

).

Since your system is listed in **Annex III** (e.g., biometrics, critical infrastructure, law enforcement, migration control), it falls under **Article 6(2)**, meaning:
- High-risk obligations (Chapter III, Sections 2–4) apply from 2 August 2026 (general application date).
- **Article 6(1)** classification (for MDR/IVDR-linked AI systems) applies from 2 August 2027 (only relevant if your AI system is also a medical device).

#### 3. Other Applicable Provisions Before 2 August 2026
- Chapters I (General Provisions) and II (Prohibited AI Practices) – Already apply from 2 February 2025 (**Article 113**, subparagraph 3, point (a) EU AI Act).
- Chapter III, Section 4 (Notified Bodies), Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models), Chapter VII (Governance), and Chapter XII (Penalties) – Apply from 2 August 2025 (

> Chapter III Section 4, Chapter V, Chapter VII and Chapter XII and Article 78 shall apply from 2 August 2025, with the exception of Article 101;

).

### Summary of Key Dates
| Provision | Applicability Date | Relevant for Your System? |
|--------------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Article 5** (Prohibited AI Practices) | 2 February 2025 | ✅ Yes (must comply) |
| Chapters I & II (General Provisions & Prohibitions) | 2 February 2025 | ✅ Yes |
| Chapter III, Section 4 (Notified Bodies), Chapter V (GPAI), Chapter VII (Governance), Chapter XII (Penalties) | 2 August 2025 | ✅ Yes (if applicable) |
| General application (**Annex III** high-risk obligations, e.g., conformity assessment, risk management, post-market monitoring) | 2 August 2026 | ✅ Yes (full compliance required) |
| **Article 6(1)** (MDR/IVDR-linked AI systems) | 2 August 2027 | ❌ Only if your AI system is also a medical device |

### Conclusion
- Today (as of 2026-07-16), only **Article 5** prohibitions and Chapters I–II apply to your system.
- From 2 August 2026, all high-risk obligations (e.g., conformity assessment, risk management, technical documentation, post-market surveillance) will apply.
- From 2 August 2027, if your AI system is also a medical device, **Article 6(1)** classification (MDR/IVDR conformity assessment) will apply.

Would you like further clarification on any specific obligation?

### Applicability of the EU AI Act to High-Risk AI Systems Listed in Annex III

#### 1. Currently Applicable Provisions (as of 2024-08-01)
The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) entered into force on 2024-08-01, but most obligations are subject to phased application. However, two key sets of provisions already apply today to providers, deployers, and other actors in the AI value chain:

##### (a) General Provisions and Prohibited AI Practices (Chapter I & II)
- Applicable since: 2025-02-02 (**Article 113**(a)).
- Key obligations:
 - Prohibited AI practices (**Article 5**): Certain AI systems (e.g., social scoring, manipulative techniques, untargeted scraping of facial images) are banned from being placed on the market, put into service, or used in the EU.
 - General provisions (Chapter I): Definitions (**Article 3**), scope (**Article 2**), and exemptions (e.g., national security, military use) apply.
 - No high-risk obligations yet: The full set of high-risk requirements (e.g., conformity assessment, risk management, technical documentation) do not apply until 2026-08-02.

##### (b) Governance, Notified Bodies, and Penalties (Chapters V, VII, XII)
- Applicable since: 2025-08-02 (**Article 113**(b)).
- Key obligations:
 - Notified bodies (Chapter III, Section 4): Designation and oversight of conformity assessment bodies for high-risk AI systems.
 - General-purpose AI models (Chapter V): Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models (e.g., transparency, risk management).
 - Governance (Chapter VII): Establishment of the AI Office and national competent authorities.
 - Penalties (Chapter XII): Administrative fines and enforcement mechanisms (**Article 99**).

---

#### 2. High-Risk Obligations (Annex III Systems)
- Applicable from: 2026-08-02 (**Article 113**(c)).
- Key obligations (Chapter III, Section 2):
 - Conformity assessment (**Article 43**): Providers must follow the prescribed procedures (e.g., internal control or notified body involvement, depending on the system).
 - Risk management (**Article 9**): Continuous identification and mitigation of risks.
 - Data governance (**Article 10**): High-quality training, validation, and testing datasets.
 - Technical documentation (**Article 11**): Comprehensive records demonstrating compliance.
 - Transparency (**Article 13**): Clear instructions for use and human oversight measures.
 - Post-market monitoring (**Article 72**): Continuous monitoring and reporting of serious incidents.
 - Registration (**Article 49**): Providers must register high-risk AI systems in the EU database before placing them on the market.

##### Exception for AI Systems Covered by Union Harmonisation Legislation (e.g., MDR/IVDR)
- If the high-risk AI system is also a medical device (e.g., AI-driven diagnostic software), **Article 43(3)** applies:
 - The MDR/IVDR conformity assessment procedure takes precedence.
 - The AI Act’s requirements (Section 2 of Chapter III) are integrated into the existing conformity assessment under the sectoral legislation.
 - No separate AI Act conformity assessment is required, but the AI-specific obligations (e.g., risk management, transparency) must still be fulfilled.

---

#### 3. Summary of Key Dates
| Provision | Applicability Date | Governing Article |
|----------------------------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Prohibited AI practices (**Article 5**) | 2025-02-02 | **Article 113**(a) |
| Notified bodies, governance, penalties | 2025-08-02 | **Article 113**(b) |
| High-risk obligations (**Annex III**) | 2026-08-02 | **Article 113**(c) |
| High-risk classification (**Annex I**) | 2027-08-02 | **Article 113**(c) |

---

#### 4. Immediate Actions for Providers of Annex III High-Risk AI Systems
1. Monitor prohibited practices (**Article 5**): Ensure the system does not fall under any banned categories (e.g., social scoring, manipulative AI).
2. Prepare for governance structures: Familiarise yourself with the AI Office and national competent authorities (applicable from 2025-08-02).
3. Plan for high-risk compliance (2026-08-02):
 - Conduct a gap analysis against Chapter III, Section 2 requirements.
 - Establish risk management, data governance, and technical documentation processes.
 - Prepare for conformity assessment (internal control or notified body involvement).
4. Check for sectoral overlaps (e.g., MDR/IVDR):
 - If the AI system is also a medical device, ensure compliance with MDR/IVDR and integrate AI Act requirements into the existing conformity assessment (**Article 43(3)**).

---

#### 5. Legal Basis
- Scope and applicability:**Article 2** (defines actors subject to the AI Act) and **Article 113** (phased application).
- High-risk obligations:**Article 6(2)** (**Annex III** systems) and Chapter III, Section 2 (requirements for high-risk AI systems).
- Conformity assessment:**Article 43** (procedures for high-risk AI systems).
- Sectoral integration (e.g., MDR):**Article 43(3)** (priority of Union harmonisation legislation).

---

### Obligations Under the EU AI Act for Providers of Non-High-Risk AI Systems

Your AI-powered contract-analysis tool is classified as non-high-risk under the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689). While this exempts you from the stringent requirements applicable to high-risk AI systems (Chapter III, Section 2), you remain subject to general obligations under the AI Act. Below is a breakdown of applicable and non-applicable obligations, with specific Article references.

---

### 1. Obligations THAT APPLY to You (Non-High-Risk AI System Provider)

#### (a) Transparency Obligations (Chapter IV, Articles 50–52)
Your system is not high-risk, but if it interacts with natural persons (e.g., procurement teams), transparency requirements may apply:

- **Article 50(1)** – Transparency for AI systems interacting with natural persons
 - If your system is designed to interact with natural persons (e.g., chatbots, virtual assistants), you must inform users that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the circumstances and context of use.
 - *Example*: If your tool provides automated contract summaries to procurement officers, you must disclose that the output is AI-generated.
 - Exception: If the AI system is used to detect, prevent, investigate, or prosecute criminal offences, this obligation does not apply.

- **Article 50(2)** – Transparency for emotion recognition or biometric categorisation systems
 - If your system infers emotions or classifies individuals based on biometric data, you must inform the affected persons of this functionality.
 - *Example*: If your tool analyses facial expressions or voice tone in contract negotiations (unlikely in procurement), this applies.

- **Article 50(3)** – Transparency for deep fakes and AI-generated content
 - If your system generates or manipulates image, audio, or video content (e.g., synthetic contract clauses), you must disclose that the content is AI-generated, unless it is obvious or used for artistic, creative, or satirical purposes.
 - *Example*: If your tool auto-generates contract amendments, users must be informed that the text was AI-generated.

- **Article 50(4)** – Transparency for general-purpose AI models (if applicable)
 - If your system integrates a general-purpose AI model (e.g., a large language model for contract analysis), you must comply with the transparency obligations in **Article 53** (e.g., providing technical documentation, summaries of training data).

#### (b) Prohibited AI Practices (Chapter II, Article 5)
- **Article 5** – Prohibited AI practices
 - You must not deploy AI systems that:
 - Use subliminal techniques to distort behaviour (**Article 5(1)**(a)).
 - Exploit vulnerabilities of specific groups (e.g., age, disability) to cause harm (**Article 5(1)**(b)).
 - Enable social scoring by public authorities (**Article 5(1)**(c)).
 - Use real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with exceptions) (**Article 5(1)**(d)).
 - Predict criminal behaviour based solely on profiling (**Article 5(1)**(e)).
 - Scrape facial images for biometric databases without consent (**Article 5(1)**(f)).
 - Infer emotions in workplaces or educational institutions (with exceptions) (**Article 5(1)**(g)).
 - Categorise individuals based on sensitive attributes (e.g., race, political opinions) (**Article 5(1)**(h)).
 - *Relevance*: If your contract-analysis tool inadvertently infers emotions or makes biased decisions, it could violate **Article 5(1)**(b) or (h).

#### (c) General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Compliance (Article 10 AI Act + GDPR)
- **Article 10**(1–5) – Data governance for high-risk AI systems
 - *Does not apply to non-high-risk systems*, but GDPR still applies to any personal data processed by your tool.
 - If your system processes personal data (e.g., procurement officers’ names, contract signatories), you must comply with GDPR (Regulation 2016/679), including:
 - Lawful basis for processing (consent, contract, legitimate interest).
 - Data minimisation (only collect necessary data).
 - Right to explanation (if automated decisions are made, users may request human review under GDPR **Article 22**).

#### (d) Market Surveillance and Compliance (Chapter IX, Articles 71–80)
- **Article 71** – EU database for high-risk AI systems
 - *Does not apply* (only for high-risk systems).
 - However, market surveillance authorities may still monitor your system under **Article 80** if they suspect misclassification.

- **Article 80** – Procedure for dealing with AI systems classified as non-high-risk
 - If a market surveillance authority believes your system should be classified as high-risk, they may:
 - Evaluate your classification (**Article 80(1)**).
 - Require corrective actions if they find it is high-risk (**Article 80(2)**).
 - Impose fines if misclassification was intentional (**Article 80(7)**).
 - *Key takeaway*: You must document your classification rationale to defend against potential challenges.

#### (e) Post-Market Monitoring (PMM) and Incident Reporting (Limited Applicability)
- **Article 72** – Post-market monitoring (PMM) for high-risk AI systems
 - *Does not apply* (only for high-risk systems).
 - However, general product safety laws (e.g., GPSR 2023/988) may still require vigilance and incident reporting if your tool causes harm.

- **Article 73** – Serious incident reporting for high-risk AI systems
 - *Does not apply* (only for high-risk systems).
 - However, if your system causes material harm (e.g., incorrect contract analysis leading to financial loss), sectoral laws (e.g., MDR if it qualifies as a medical device, GDPR if personal data is breached) may require reporting.

#### (f) Cooperation with Competent Authorities (Article 21)
- **Article 21** – Cooperation with competent authorities
 - If a national competent authority (e.g., AI Office, market surveillance authority) requests information about your system, you must provide documentation to demonstrate compliance (**Article 21(1)**).
 - *Example*: If authorities suspect your system is high-risk, they may request technical documentation to verify your classification.

---

### 2. Obligations THAT DO NOT APPLY to You (Reserved for High-Risk AI Systems)

Since your system is not high-risk, the following Chapter III (High-Risk AI Systems) obligations do not apply:

| Obligation | Governing Article | Why It Does Not Apply |
|----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Conformity assessment | **Article 43** | Only high-risk AI systems require third-party conformity assessment (e.g., via notified bodies). |
| Quality management system (QMS) | **Article 17** | High-risk providers must implement a QMS covering risk management, data governance, and post-market monitoring. |
| Technical documentation | **Article 11** | High-risk systems must maintain detailed technical documentation (e.g., system design, risk assessments). |
| Automated logging | **Article 12** | High-risk systems must automatically log events (e.g., inputs, outputs, errors) for traceability. |
| Human oversight | **Article 14** | High-risk systems must enable human oversight (e.g., "stop button" for deployers). |
| Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity | **Article 15** | High-risk systems must meet strict performance and security standards. |
| EU declaration of conformity | **Article 47** | High-risk systems must draw up an EU DoC and affix CE marking. |
| Registration in EU database | **Article 49** | High-risk systems must be registered in the EU AI database before market placement. |
| Post-market monitoring (PMM) system | **Article 72** | High-risk providers must actively monitor system performance post-deployment. |
| Serious incident reporting | **Article 73** | High-risk providers must report serious incidents to authorities. |
| Corrective actions (recall, withdrawal) | **Article 20** | High-risk providers must take corrective actions (e.g., recalls) if non-conformities are found. |
| Authorised representative (for non-EU providers) | **Article 22** | Non-EU high-risk providers must appoint an EU authorised representative. |

---

### 3. Key Takeaways for Compliance

#### ✅ What You MUST Do (Non-High-Risk Provider Obligations)
1. Ensure transparency (**Article 50**) if your system:
 - Interacts with natural persons (e.g., chatbots, automated responses).
 - Generates or manipulates content (e.g., synthetic contract clauses).
 - Uses emotion recognition or biometric categorisation (unlikely in procurement).
2. Avoid prohibited AI practices (**Article 5**), such as:
 - Exploiting vulnerabilities (e.g., biased contract recommendations).
 - Social scoring or predictive policing (irrelevant for procurement).
3. Comply with GDPR if processing personal data.
4. Document your classification rationale to defend against **Article 80** challenges.
5. Cooperate with authorities if they request information (**Article 21**).

#### ❌ What You DO NOT Need to Do (High-Risk Exemptions)
- No conformity assessment (**Article 43**).
- No CE marking or EU declaration of conformity (**Article 47**).
- No registration in the EU AI database (**Article 49**).
- No mandatory logging or human oversight (**Articles 12**, 14).
- No post-market monitoring (PMM) or serious incident reporting (**Articles 72**, 73).

---

### 4. Risk Mitigation Recommendations
- Monitor regulatory developments: The AI Act’s **Annex III** (high-risk use cases) may expand, potentially bringing contract-analysis tools into scope in the future.
- Voluntary compliance with high-risk standards: Even if not mandatory, adopting ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems) or NIST AI Risk Management Framework can enhance trust and prepare for future regulation.
- Internal audits: Periodically review whether your system could be reclassified as high-risk (e.g., if it starts making automated contract approval decisions).
- User agreements: Clearly state that your tool is not a substitute for legal advice to limit liability.

---

### 5. Summary Table

| Obligation Type | Applies to You? | Governing Article(s) | Key Requirement |
|----------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Transparency | ✅ Yes | **Article 50** | Disclose AI interaction, content generation, or biometric categorisation. |
| Prohibited AI Practices | ✅ Yes | **Article 5** | Avoid subliminal manipulation, social scoring, or biased decision-making. |
| GDPR Compliance | ✅ Yes | GDPR 2016/679 | Ensure lawful processing of personal data. |
| Market Surveillance Cooperation | ✅ Yes | **Article 21**, 80 | Provide documentation if authorities challenge your classification. |
| Conformity Assessment | ❌ No | **Article 43** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Quality Management System (QMS) | ❌ No | **Article 17** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Technical Documentation | ❌ No | **Article 11** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Automated Logging | ❌ No | **Article 12** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Human Oversight | ❌ No | **Article 14** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| EU Declaration of Conformity | ❌ No | **Article 47** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Registration in EU Database | ❌ No | **Article 49** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Post-Market Monitoring (PMM) | ❌ No | **Article 72** | Only for high-risk systems. |
| Serious Incident Reporting | ❌ No | **Article 73** | Only for high-risk systems. |

---

### 6. Next Steps for Compliance
1. Review transparency requirements (**Article 50**) and implement user disclosures if applicable.
2. Ensure GDPR compliance if processing personal data.
3. Document your classification rationale to defend against **Article 80** challenges.
4. Monitor for updates to **Annex III** (high-risk use cases) that could affect your tool.
5. Consider voluntary adoption of AI best practices (e.g., ISO/IEC 42001) to future-proof compliance.

If your tool evolves to include automated decision-making (e.g., contract approval/rejection), reassess whether it qualifies as high-risk under **Article 6(2)** (**Annex III**, point 5 – […]).

> - “AI systems intended to be used for the purpose of making decisions on the access to, or the assignment of, public and...”

### Obligations Under the EU AI Act for Providers of Non-High-Risk AI Systems

Your AI-powered contract-analysis tool is not classified as a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689). However, as its provider, you remain subject to certain general obligations and transparency requirements under the AI Act. Below is a breakdown of applicable obligations and requirements that do not apply to you because they are reserved for high-risk AI systems.

---

### 1. Obligations That APPLY to You (Non-High-Risk AI System Provider)

#### (a) General Transparency Obligations (Chapter IV, Article 50)
Your tool is an AI system intended to interact directly with natural persons (e.g., procurement teams). Therefore, you must ensure that users are informed they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the context of use.

- **Article 50(1)** requires:
 > *"Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons are designed and developed in such a way that the natural persons concerned are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the point of view of a natural person who is reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect, taking into account the circumstances and the context of use."*

 - Key takeaway: If your tool interacts with users in a way that is not clearly identifiable as AI-driven, you must disclose this explicitly (e.g., via a disclaimer, UI notification, or documentation).
 - Exception: If the AI interaction is obviously automated (e.g., a chatbot with a robotic avatar), no additional disclosure is required.

- **Article 50(5)** specifies that this information must be provided at the latest at the time of the first interaction and must comply with accessibility requirements.

#### (b) General Prohibitions on AI Practices (Chapter II, Article 5)
Even if your system is not high-risk, it must not engage in any of the prohibited AI practices listed in **Article 5**, such as:
- Social scoring (e.g., evaluating individuals based on behavior for general purposes).
- Subliminal manipulation (e.g., exploiting vulnerabilities to distort behavior).
- Exploitative AI targeting vulnerable groups (e.g., children, persons with disabilities).
- Real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with limited exceptions for law enforcement).

- Key takeaway: Review your tool’s functionality to ensure it does not fall under any of these prohibitions. If it does, it is illegal regardless of risk classification.

#### (c) Obligations for General-Purpose AI (GPAI) Systems (If Applicable)
If your contract-analysis tool is built on a general-purpose AI model (e.g., a large language model like GPT-4), additional obligations under Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models) may apply, including:
- Transparency requirements (**Article 53**).
- Risk management and documentation obligations (**Article 55**).
- Compliance with copyright law (**Article 53(1)**(c)).

- Key takeaway: If your tool is not built on a GPAI model, these obligations do not apply. If it is, you must comply with Chapter V even if the system itself is not high-risk.

#### (d) Data Governance and Fundamental Rights (If Processing Personal Data)
While not an AI Act-specific obligation, if your tool processes personal data, you must comply with the GDPR (Regulation 2016/679). The AI Act does not replace GDPR but may impose additional transparency requirements (e.g., **Article 50(3)** for emotion recognition or biometric categorization, if applicable).

- Key takeaway: If your tool processes personal data, ensure GDPR compliance (e.g., lawful basis, data minimization, user rights).

---

### 2. Obligations That DO NOT APPLY to You (Reserved for High-Risk AI Systems)

Since your tool is not high-risk, the following Chapter III obligations (applicable only to high-risk AI systems) do not apply:

| Obligation | Governing Article | Why It Does Not Apply |
|----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Conformity assessment | **Article 16**(f), **Article 43** | High-risk AI systems must undergo third-party conformity assessment before being placed on the market. Your tool is exempt. |
| Quality management system | **Article 16**(c), **Article 17** | High-risk providers must implement a quality management system (QMS). Your tool is exempt. |
| Technical documentation & logs | **Article 16**(d), **Article 18**, **Article 19** | High-risk providers must maintain detailed technical documentation and automatically generated logs for 10 years. Your tool is exempt. |
| EU declaration of conformity & CE marking | **Article 16**(g), **Article 47**, **Article 48** | High-risk AI systems must have an EU declaration of conformity and CE marking. Your tool is exempt. |
| Registration in EU database | **Article 16**(i), **Article 49** | High-risk AI systems must be registered in the EU database before market placement. Your tool is exempt. |
| Corrective actions & incident reporting | **Article 16**(j), **Article 20** | High-risk providers must report serious incidents and take corrective actions (e.g., recalls). Your tool is exempt. |
| Human oversight requirements | **Article 14**, **Article 26** | High-risk AI systems must be designed for human oversight, and deployers must assign competent personnel for monitoring. Your tool is exempt. |
| Fundamental rights impact assessment | **Article 27** | Deployers of high-risk AI systems in public services or law enforcement must conduct a fundamental rights impact assessment. Your tool is exempt. |
| Obligations for distributors & importers | **Article 24**, **Article 23** | Distributors and importers of high-risk AI systems have specific verification and reporting duties. Your tool is exempt. |
| Authorized representative requirements | **Article 22** | Non-EU providers of high-risk AI systems must appoint an EU-authorized representative. Your tool is exempt. |

---

### 3. Key Considerations for Your Compliance Strategy

#### (a) Voluntary Compliance with High-Risk Requirements (If Beneficial)
While not legally required, you may voluntarily adopt certain high-risk obligations (e.g., technical documentation, logging, or transparency measures) to:
- Enhance trust with customers (e.g., by providing instructions for use as per **Article 13**).
- Prepare for future regulatory changes (e.g., if your tool is later classified as high-risk).
- Mitigate liability risks (e.g., by maintaining audit logs for dispute resolution).

#### (b) Monitoring for Future High-Risk Classification
The AI Act allows for updates to **Annex III** (which lists high-risk AI systems). If your tool’s intended purpose evolves (e.g., if it starts making automated decisions with legal or financial consequences), it could later be classified as high-risk. You should:
- Monitor updates to **Annex III** (via the AI Office or European Commission).
- Document your risk assessment (as per **Article 6(4)**) to justify why your tool is not high-risk.

#### (c) Sector-Specific Regulations (If Applicable)
If your tool is used in regulated sectors (e.g., financial services, healthcare, or public procurement), additional sectoral laws may apply (e.g., MiFID II, GDPR, or public procurement rules). The AI Act does not override these but may complement them.

---

### 4. Summary Table: Applicable vs. Non-Applicable Obligations

| Category | Applicable Obligations (Non-High-Risk) | Non-Applicable Obligations (High-Risk Only) |
|-------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Transparency | **Article 50(1)** – Disclose AI interaction (if not obvious) | **Article 13** – Detailed instructions for use |
| Prohibitions | **Article 5** – No social scoring, subliminal manipulation, etc. | **Article 26** – Human oversight requirements |
| General-Purpose AI (if applicable) | Chapter V (**Articles 53**-55) – Transparency, risk management, copyright compliance | **Article 16** – Full high-risk provider obligations |
| Data Protection | GDPR (2016/679) – Lawful processing, user rights | **Article 27** – Fundamental rights impact assessment |
| Market Surveillance | General market surveillance rules (Regulation 2019/1020) | **Article 20** – Mandatory incident reporting & corrective actions |
| Documentation & Logging | No legal requirement (but recommended for liability protection) | **Article 18**, **Article 19** – Technical documentation & logs for 10 years |
| Conformity Assessment | Not required | **Article 43** – Third-party conformity assessment |
| CE Marking & Registration | Not required | **Article 47**, **Article 48**, **Article 49** – EU declaration, CE marking, EU database registration |

---

### 5. Recommended Next Steps
1. Confirm non-high-risk status:
 - Document your risk assessment (as per **Article 6(4)**) to justify why your tool is not high-risk.
 - Monitor **Annex III** updates to ensure future compliance.

2. Implement transparency measures (**Article 50(1)**):
 - If your tool interacts with users in a non-obvious AI-driven way, add a disclosure (e.g., "This tool uses AI to analyze contracts").
 - Ensure the disclosure is clear, accessible, and provided at first interaction.

3. Review for prohibited AI practices (**Article 5**):
 - Ensure your tool does not engage in social scoring, subliminal manipulation, or exploitative AI.

4. Check for General-Purpose AI (GPAI) obligations (if applicable):
 - If your tool is built on a GPAI model, comply with Chapter V (**Articles 53**-55).

5. Maintain GDPR compliance (if processing personal data):
 - Ensure lawful basis, data minimization, and user rights are respected.

6. Consider voluntary adoption of high-risk best practices:
 - Technical documentation (for internal audits).
 - Logging (for dispute resolution).
 - User instructions (to enhance transparency).

---

### 6. Key Takeaways
✅ You must comply with:
- **Article 50(1)** (transparency for AI interactions).
- **Article 5** (prohibited AI practices).
- GDPR (if processing personal data).
- Chapter V (if using a GPAI model).

❌ You are exempt from:
- All high-risk obligations (Chapter III, **Articles 16**-27).
- Conformity assessment, CE marking, and EU database registration.
- Mandatory incident reporting and corrective actions.

🔍 Monitor for future changes:
- **Annex III** updates (could reclassify your tool as high-risk).
- Sectoral regulations (e.g., financial services, healthcare).

By following this framework, you can ensure full compliance with the EU AI Act while avoiding unnecessary obligations reserved for high-risk systems.

---
> **Scope & limitations**
> - Retrieval coverage for this question was partial — some relevant provisions may not have been surfaced; confirm against the full text.

full.answer:
### Responsibilities Along the AI Value Chain Under the EU AI Act

Under the EU AI Act, the relationship between your company (as a downstream provider integrating a third-party general-purpose AI (GPAI) model) and the GPAI model provider is governed by **Article 25(4)** and **Article 53(1)**, point (b). Below is a breakdown of the obligations and the information you can expect to receive:

---

### 1. Obligations Between Downstream Providers and GPAI Model Providers
#### a. Written Agreement Requirement (Article 25(4))
- **Article 25(4)** requires that the provider of a high-risk AI system (which may include your SaaS product if it qualifies as high-risk under **Article 6**) and the third-party supplier of the GPAI model must enter into a written agreement specifying:
 - The necessary information, capabilities, technical access, and other assistance (based on the generally acknowledged state of the art) to enable you to fully comply with your obligations under the AI Act.
 - This ensures that you, as the downstream provider, receive the technical and operational support needed to integrate the GPAI model while meeting regulatory requirements.

- Exception for Open-Source Models (**Article 25(4)**, subparagraph 1):
 - This obligation does not apply if the GPAI model is made available under a free and open-source licence (unless it presents systemic risks).

#### b. Role of the GPAI Model Provider (Article 53(1), point (b))
- The GPAI model provider must:
 - Draw up, keep up-to-date, and make availableinformation and documentation to downstream providers (like your company) who intend to integrate the model into their AI systems.
 - This documentation must:
 - (i) Enable you to understand the model’s capabilities and limitations and comply with your obligations under the AI Act (e.g., risk management, transparency, technical robustness).
 - (ii) Contain, at a minimum, the elements set out in **Annex XII** (see below).

- Confidentiality Safeguards (**Article 53(1)**, point (b)):
 - The obligation to provide documentation is without prejudice to intellectual property rights, confidential business information, or trade secrets (in line with Union and national law).

---

### 2. Information and Documentation You Can Expect from the GPAI Model Provider
The GPAI model provider must provide you with technical documentation as specified in **Annex XII**, which includes:

#### a. General Description of the GPAI Model (Annex XII, point 1)
- The tasks the model is intended to perform and the type/nature of AI systems into which it can be integrated.
- Acceptable use policies (to ensure compliance with intended purposes).
- Date of release and methods of distribution (e.g., API, downloadable weights).
- How the model interacts with hardware/software (if applicable).
- Versions of relevant software (e.g., dependencies, frameworks).
- Model architecture and number of parameters.
- Modality (e.g., text, image) and format of inputs/outputs.
- Licence terms (to clarify usage rights).

#### b. Detailed Description of Model Elements and Development Process (Annex XII, point 2)
- Technical means required for integration (e.g., instructions for use, infrastructure, tools).
- Modality, format, and maximum size of inputs/outputs (e.g., context window length).
- Information on training, testing, and validation data (where applicable), including:
 - Type and provenance of data (e.g., public datasets, proprietary sources).
 - Curation methodologies (e.g., cleaning, filtering, bias detection measures).

---

### 3. Additional Considerations
#### a. Free and Open-Source Exemption (Article 53(2))
- If the GPAI model is released under a free and open-source licence (with publicly available parameters, architecture, and usage information), the provider is exempt from the obligations in **Article 53(1)**, points (a) and (b) (i.e., no requirement to provide technical documentation or transparency information).
- Exception: This exemption does not apply if the model presents systemic risks (as defined in **Article 3(65)** and **Article 51**).

#### b. Systemic Risk Models (Article 55)
- If the GPAI model is classified as having systemic risk, the provider must comply with additional obligations under **Article 55**, including:
 - Model evaluation and adversarial testing (to identify and mitigate systemic risks).
 - Risk assessment and mitigation measures.
 - Incident reporting (to the AI Office and national authorities).
 - Cybersecurity protections.
- As a downstream provider, you should verify whether the model is listed as systemic-risk (published by the Commission under **Article 52(6)**).

#### c. Enforcement and Compliance (Articles 89, 91, 92)
- **Article 89(2)** grants you, as a downstream provider, the right to lodge a complaint with the AI Office if you suspect the GPAI model provider is infringing the AI Act.
- The AI Office may request documentation (**Article 91**) or conduct evaluations (**Article 92**) to assess compliance.

---

### Summary of Key Provisions
| Obligation | Governing Article/Annex | Key Requirements |
|----------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Written agreement between downstream provider and GPAI model provider | **Article 25(4)** | Specifies information, technical access, and support needed for compliance. |
| GPAI model provider’s obligation to provide documentation | **Article 53(1)**, point (b) | Must enable downstream providers to understand model capabilities/limitations and comply with the AI Act. |
| Minimum content of documentation | **Annex XII** | Includes model description, integration requirements, data provenance, and usage policies. |
| Exemption for free/open-source models | **Article 53(2)** | No documentation obligation unless the model presents systemic risks. |
| Additional obligations for systemic-risk models | **Article 55** | Model evaluation, risk mitigation, incident reporting, and cybersecurity. |
| Right to lodge complaints | **Article 89(2)** | Downstream providers can report suspected infringements. |

---

### Conclusion
As a downstream provider integrating a GPAI model, you are entitled to receive technical documentation from the model provider under **Article 53(1)**, point (b) and **Annex XII**, enabling you to understand the model’s capabilities, limitations, and integration requirements. You must also ensure a written agreement with the provider (under **Article 25(4)**) to secure the necessary support for compliance. If the model is open-source, the provider may be exempt from these obligations unless it presents systemic risks.

### Responsibilities Along the AI Value Chain Under the EU AI Act

Under the EU AI Act, the responsibilities between your company (as a downstream provider integrating a third-party general-purpose AI (GPAI) model) and the GPAI model provider are governed by the following provisions:

---

### 1. Your Role as a Downstream Provider
You qualify as a downstream provider under **Article 3(16)**, meaning you integrate a GPAI model into your AI system (your SaaS product). While the primary obligations under Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models) rest with the GPAI model provider, your responsibilities include:
- Ensuring compliance with **Article 25(4)** (if your AI system becomes high-risk due to integration of the GPAI model).
- Cooperating with the GPAI provider to obtain necessary documentation and information (as required by **Article 53(1)**(b)).

---

### 2. Obligations of the GPAI Model Provider Toward You
The GPAI model provider must:
#### (a) Provide Technical Documentation and Information (Article 53(1)(b))
The provider must draw up, keep up-to-date, and make available information and documentation to enable you (as a downstream provider) to:
- Understand the capabilities and limitations of the GPAI model (**Article 53(1)**(b)(i)).
- Comply with your obligations under the AI Act (**Article 53(1)**(b)(i)).

The minimum required information is set out in **Annex XII**, which includes:
- A general description of the model (tasks, acceptable use policies, architecture, input/output modalities, license) (**Annex XII**, point 1).
- Technical means required for integration (e.g., instructions, infrastructure, tools) (**Annex XII**, point 2(a)).
- Data used for training/testing/validation (type, provenance, curation methodologies) (**Annex XII**, point 2(c)).

#### (b) Exceptions for Open-Source Models (Article 53(2))
If the GPAI model is released under a free and open-source license with publicly available parameters, the provider is exempt from providing technical documentation (**Article 53(1)**(a) and (b)), unless the model presents systemic risks (**Article 53(2)**).

#### (c) Additional Obligations for Systemic-Risk Models (Article 55)
If the GPAI model is classified as having systemic risk, the provider must also:
- Perform model evaluations (including adversarial testing) (**Article 55(1)**(a)).
- Assess and mitigate systemic risks (**Article 55(1)**(b)).
- Report serious incidents to the AI Office (**Article 55(1)**(c)).

---

### 3. Your Right to Lodge Complaints (Article 89(2))
If the GPAI provider fails to comply with its obligations (e.g., not providing required documentation), you have the right to lodge a complaint with the AI Office, alleging an infringement of the AI Act. The complaint must include:
- The provider’s contact details (**Article 89(2)**(a)).
- A description of the alleged infringement (**Article 89(2)**(b)).
- Any supporting evidence (**Article 89(2)**(c)).

---

### 4. Contractual Agreements (Article 25(4))
If your AI system becomes high-risk due to integration of the GPAI model, you and the provider must specify in writing the necessary information, technical access, and assistance to ensure compliance with the AI Act (**Article 25(4)**). This includes:
- Information on the GPAI model’s capabilities and limitations.
- Technical access to enable compliance with high-risk AI system requirements.

Exception: If the GPAI model is made publicly available under a free and open-source license, this obligation does not apply (**Article 25(4)**, second subparagraph).

---

### Summary of Key Provisions
| Provision | Obligation | Applies to |
|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Article 53(1)**(b) | GPAI provider must provide documentation to downstream providers | All GPAI models (unless open-source without systemic risk) |
| **Annex XII** | Minimum content of documentation (e.g., model description, integration tools, training data) | All GPAI models (unless open-source without systemic risk) |
| **Article 55** | Additional obligations for systemic-risk GPAI models (e.g., adversarial testing, incident reporting) | GPAI models with systemic risk |
| **Article 89(2)** | Downstream providers can lodge complaints against non-compliant GPAI providers | All GPAI models |
| **Article 25(4)** | Written agreement on information sharing if the AI system becomes high-risk | High-risk AI systems integrating GPAI models |

---

### Conclusion
As a downstream provider, you can expect the GPAI model provider to furnish:
1. Technical documentation (per **Annex XII**) to help you understand and integrate the model.
2. Information on capabilities, limitations, and compliance requirements (per **Article 53(1)**(b)).
3. Additional risk-related documentation if the model has systemic risk (per **Article 55**).

If the provider fails to comply, you may lodge a complaint under **Article 89(2)**. If your AI system becomes high-risk, you must also formalize information-sharing agreements under **Article 25(4)**.

full.answer:
### 1. When is consent NOT a valid legal basis due to an imbalance of power?
Under the GDPR, consent is not a valid legal basis for processing personal data—including health data—where there is a clear imbalance of power between the controller (here, the hospital) and the data subject (the patient). This is explicitly addressed in **Recital 43**, which states:

> *"Consent is presumed not to be freely given if it does not allow separate consent to be given to different personal data processing operations despite it being appropriate in the individual case, or if the performance of a contract, including the provision of a service, is dependent on the consent despite such consent not being necessary for such performance."*Key triggers for invalidity due to imbalance:
- The controller is a public authority (e.g., a public hospital), making it unlikely that consent was freely given in all circumstances.
- The data subject has no genuine or free choice (e.g., a patient may feel compelled to consent to avoid perceived negative consequences for their treatment).
- Consent is bundled with other matters (e.g., tied to the provision of healthcare services) where it is not strictly necessary for performance.

Regulatory basis:
- **Recital 43** (interpretive, not operative) clarifies that consent is presumed invalid in cases of imbalance.
- **Article 7(4)** (binding) reinforces this by requiring that consent must not be conditional on the performance of a contract if the processing is not necessary for that contract.

Conclusion for the hospital scenario:
If the hospital makes access to its diagnostic platform (or broader healthcare services) conditional on consent to data processing that is not strictly necessary for the provision of care, the consent is not valid under the GDPR. This is particularly relevant for health data, which are classified as special categories of personal data under **Article 9(1)**.

---

### 2. Alternative legal bases under Article 9 for processing health data
Since **Article 9(1)** prohibits the processing of health data unless an exception applies, the hospital must rely on one of the explicit derogations in **Article 9(2)**. The most relevant alternatives to consent in this setting are:

#### (a) Processing necessary for healthcare purposes (Article 9(2)(h))
- Applicability: Processing is necessary for:
 - Preventive or occupational medicine,
 - Medical diagnosis,
 - Provision of health or social care or treatment, or
 - Management of health or social care systems/services.
- Conditions:
 - Processing must be based on Union/Member State law or pursuant to a contract with a health professional.
 - The processor must be subject to professional secrecy (e.g., doctors, nurses, or other regulated health professionals) under **Article 9(3)**.
- Example: Using the AI diagnostic platform to assist clinicians in diagnosing patients as part of routine care.

#### (b) Processing for reasons of public interest in public health (Article 9(2)(i))
- Applicability: Processing is necessary for:
 - Protecting against serious cross-border threats to health, or
 - Ensuring high standards of quality and safety of healthcare, medicinal products, or medical devices.
- Conditions:
 - Must be based on Union/Member State law.
 - Must include suitable safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation, access controls) to protect patient rights.
- Example: Using anonymised/pseudonymised health data to monitor disease outbreaks or improve diagnostic algorithms for public health purposes.

#### (c) Processing for scientific research purposes (Article 9(2)(j))
- Applicability: Processing is necessary for:
 - Scientific or historical research purposes, or
 - Statistical purposes.
- Conditions:
 - Must comply with **Article 89(1)**, which requires:
 - Appropriate safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation, data minimisation).
 - Union/Member State law permitting derogations from certain data subject rights (e.g., **Articles 15**, 16, 18, 21) if those rights would render the research impossible or seriously impair it.
- Example: Using patient data to train or validate the AI diagnostic platform for research purposes, provided safeguards are in place.

#### (d) Explicit consent (Article 9(2)(a)) – Only if valid
- Applicability: The data subject has given explicit consent to the processing for one or more specified purposes.
- Conditions:
 - Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous (per **Article 4(11)** and **Article 7**).
 - No imbalance of power must exist (see Section 1 above).
 - Union/Member State law must not prohibit the lifting of the **Article 9(1)** prohibition via consent.
- Caveat: Given the hospital-patient power dynamic, explicit consent is unlikely to be valid unless the patient has a genuine choice (e.g., opting into a non-essential research study with no impact on their care).

---

### 3. Recommended approach for the hospital
1. Avoid relying on consent unless the hospital can demonstrate that:
 - Patients have a genuine, free choice (e.g., the AI platform is optional and does not affect their care).
 - Consent is unbundled from other services (e.g., not tied to treatment).
 - The hospital is not a public authority (or, if it is, that Union/Member State law explicitly permits consent as a basis).

2. Prefer **Article 9(2)**(h) (healthcare purposes) if the AI platform is used for:
 - Medical diagnosis or treatment (e.g., assisting clinicians in interpreting scans).
 - Management of health services (e.g., improving workflows).
 - Ensure processing is limited to what is necessary and performed by professionals bound by secrecy.

3. Consider **Article 9(2)**(i) (public health) if the AI platform is used for:
 - Public health monitoring (e.g., tracking disease trends).
 - Quality/safety improvements (e.g., validating diagnostic accuracy).
 - Ensure Union/Member State law authorises the processing and safeguards are in place.

4. Use **Article 9(2)**(j) (research purposes) if the AI platform is used for:
 - Scientific research (e.g., training algorithms on pseudonymised data).
 - Ensure compliance with **Article 89(1)** (e.g., pseudonymisation, data minimisation) and Union/Member State law permitting derogations from data subject rights.

---

### 4. Key GDPR obligations regardless of legal basis
- Transparency: Provide patients with clear information about the processing (per **Articles 13**–14).
- Data minimisation: Process only the minimum necessary data for the specified purpose.
- Security: Implement appropriate technical/organisational measures (per **Article 32**).
- Data subject rights: Facilitate rights such as access (**Article 15**), rectification (**Article 16**), and erasure (**Article 17**), unless derogations apply (e.g., for research under **Article 89**).
- Records of processing: Maintain records under **Article 30** if the hospital has 250+ employees or processes high-risk data.

### 1. When is consent NOT a valid legal basis due to an imbalance of power?
Under the GDPR, consent is not a valid legal basis for processing personal data where there is a clear imbalance of power between the controller and the data subject, particularly when the controller is a public authority or in a position of authority over the data subject (e.g., employer-employee, healthcare provider-patient). This is explicitly addressed in **Recital 43**, which states:

> *"Consent is presumed not to be freely given if it does not allow separate consent to be given to different personal data processing operations despite it being appropriate in the individual case, or if the performance of a contract, including the provision of a service, is dependent on the consent despite such consent not being necessary for such performance."*

> (43) In order to ensure that consent is freely given, consent should not provide a valid legal ground for the processing of personal data in a specific case where there is a clear imbalance between the data subject and the controller, in particular where the controller is a public authority and it is therefore unlikely that consent was freely given in all the circumstances of that specific situation. […]

In the hospital-patient relationship, the patient is in a vulnerable position due to their reliance on the hospital for medical care. If the hospital conditions the provision of healthcare services on the patient’s consent to data processing (e.g., for an AI diagnostic platform), this creates a power imbalance, making consent not freely given and thus invalid as a legal basis.

---

### 2. Alternative legal bases under Article 9 for processing health data
Since health data are special categories of personal data under **Article 9(1)**, their processing is prohibited unless an exception applies. The following alternative legal bases under **Article 9(2)** are relevant for a hospital processing patient health data for an AI diagnostic platform:

#### (a) Explicit consent (Article 9(2)(a))
- Not recommended in this setting due to the power imbalance (as discussed above).
- Even if obtained, consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous (**Article 7**).
- The patient must have a genuine choice to refuse without detriment to their care.

#### (b) Necessary for medical diagnosis, treatment, or healthcare management (Article 9(2)(h))
- Processing is necessary for the purposes of preventive or occupational medicine, medical diagnosis, the provision of health or social care or treatment, or the management of health or social care systems and services.
- This is the most likely valid basis for a hospital using an AI diagnostic platform, as it directly relates to patient care.
- Requires processing by (or under the responsibility of) a healthcare professional or another person subject to professional secrecy.

#### (c) Necessary for reasons of public interest in public health (Article 9(2)(i))
- Processing is necessary for reasons of public interest in the area of public health, such as protecting against serious cross-border threats to health or ensuring high standards of quality and safety of healthcare.
- This may apply if the AI platform is used for epidemiological research, disease surveillance, or improving healthcare standards.
- Requires a legal basis in EU or Member State law with suitable safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation, data minimisation).

#### (d) Necessary for scientific or historical research purposes (Article 9(2)(j))
- Processing is necessary for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes, or statistical purposes.
- Applies if the AI platform is used for medical research (e.g., training AI models on anonymised/pseudonymised data).
- Requires appropriate safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation, data minimisation) and must comply with **Article 89(1)**.

#### (e) Vital interests of the data subject (Article 9(2)(c))
- Processing is necessary to protect the vital interests of the data subject or another natural person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent.
- Not applicable in most routine AI diagnostic cases, as patients are typically capable of consenting.

---

### 3. Recommended approach for the hospital
- Primary legal basis:**Article 9(2)**(h) (necessary for medical diagnosis, treatment, or healthcare management).
 - The AI diagnostic platform must be directly linked to patient care (e.g., improving diagnostic accuracy, personalised treatment).
 - Processing must be carried out by (or under the responsibility of) a healthcare professional bound by confidentiality obligations.
- Secondary legal basis (if applicable):**Article 9(2)**(i) (public interest in public health) or **Article 9(2)**(j) (scientific research).
 - Requires additional safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation, ethical approval for research).
 - Must be proportionate and necessary for the stated purpose.

---

### 4. Key GDPR obligations when processing health data
- Transparency: Provide clear, concise, and accessible information to patients about data processing (**Article 13** or **Article 14**).
- Data minimisation: Only process necessary health data for the intended purpose.
- Security: Implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect health data (**Article 32**).
- Data subject rights: Ensure patients can access, rectify, erase, or object to processing (**Articles 15**–22).
- Records of processing activities: Maintain documentation of processing activities (**Article 30**).

---

### Conclusion
- Consent is not a valid legal basis for a hospital processing patient health data for an AI diagnostic platform due to the power imbalance (**Recital 43**).
- **Article 9(2)**(h) (necessary for medical diagnosis/treatment) is the most appropriate legal basis, provided the processing is directly linked to patient care and carried out by (or under the responsibility of) a healthcare professional.
- If the AI platform is used for public health or research purposes, **Article 9(2)**(i) or **Article 9(2)**(j) may apply, but additional safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation, ethical approval) are required.
